reviewStandard.meaning: Content remains review-required until claim-level source, accuracy, instructional, accessibility, bias/context, and qualified expert gates are complete.

sections.heading: Why This Chapter Matters
sections.preview: Assessment and Diagnosis accounts for 16% of the EPPP — the largest single content domain. Psychometrics is its foundation. Every question about whether a test "works" is really a question about reliability, validity, or measurement error. If you understand these three concepts deeply, you can reason your way through a

sections.heading: Classical Test Theory (CTT)
sections.preview: Classical Test Theory (CTT) rests on a deceptively simple equation: X = T + E Where X is the observed score, T is the true score (the hypothetical score you would get if measurement were perfect), and E is error (random fluctuation). Key assumptions of CTT: The expected value of error scores across repeated measurement

sections.heading: Reliability: Is the Test Consistent?
sections.preview: Reliability refers to the consistency and stability of test scores. A reliable test produces similar results under consistent conditions. Remember: reliability is necessary but not sufficient for validity — a test can be reliably wrong. Types of reliability (with coefficients): Type What It Measures Method Best For Wat
sections.diagramDescription: 🎯 The classic Target Analogy for Measurement. Reliability = Consistency (grouping of the hits). Validity = Accuracy (hitting the center bullseye). A test must be reliable before it can be valid.

sections.heading: The Standard Error of Measurement (SEM)
sections.preview: The SEM quantifies the amount of error expected in an individual's test score . It is the standard deviation of observed scores around the true score: SEM = SD × √(1 − r) Where SD is the standard deviation of the test and r is the reliability coefficient. Using the SEM to build confidence intervals: 68% CI: Score ± 1 S

sections.heading: Validity: Does the Test Measure What It Claims?
sections.preview: Validity is the degree to which evidence supports the intended interpretation of test scores for its proposed purpose. Modern psychometrics (Messick, 1995) views validity as a unitary concept — there aren't really "types" of validity, but rather different sources of evidence for validity. Sources of validity evidence:
sections.diagramDescription: 📊 Construct Validity Framework. Construct validity is the "umbrella" term asking if your measure actually captures the theoretical construct. Content validity asks if you sampled all parts of the subject. Criterion validity relies on external data (Concurrent = NOW, Predictive = FUTURE). Convergent/Discriminant validity ensures your construct correlates with similar traits but NOT with unrelated traits.

sections.heading: The Multitrait-Multimethod Matrix (MTMM)
sections.preview: Campbell and Fiske (1959) introduced the MTMM matrix as a systematic approach to evaluating convergent and discriminant validity — two essential components of construct validity. How it works: Measure multiple traits (e.g., anxiety, depression, self-esteem) Using multiple methods (e.g., self-report, clinician rating, b

sections.heading: Item Response Theory (IRT)
sections.preview: Item Response Theory (IRT) is the modern alternative to CTT that focuses on the relationship between an individual test item and the test-taker's latent ability level. Key differences from CTT: Feature CTT IRT Unit of analysis Total test Individual item Measurement precision Constant (same SEM for everyone) Variable (p

sections.heading: Ceiling and Floor Effects
sections.preview: Ceiling effect: When a substantial proportion of test-takers achieve the maximum score. The test is too easy for the population, and cannot differentiate among high performers. Scores cluster at the top. Floor effect: When a substantial proportion of test-takers score at the minimum. The test is too difficult , and can

sections.heading: Generalizability Theory
sections.preview: Generalizability Theory (G theory; Cronbach et al., 1972) extends CTT by partitioning error into multiple sources rather than treating all error as one undifferentiated lump. In CTT, error = everything that isn't true score. In G theory, you can estimate how much error comes from: Items (different questions produce dif

sections.heading: Putting It All Together: A Decision Framework
sections.preview: When the EPPP presents an assessment question, use this framework: "Is it consistent?" → This is a reliability question. Ask: what kind of consistency? Over time (test-retest), across items (internal consistency), across raters (inter-rater), across forms (parallel forms)? "Does it measure what it claims?" → This is a

sections.heading: Why This Chapter Matters
sections.preview: Intelligence testing is one of the most frequently tested topics on the EPPP. You need to know the major instruments (Wechsler scales, Stanford-Binet, Bayley), the theory that organizes them (CHC), the clinical issues (Flynn effect, malingering, ID/giftedness), and the adaptive behavior measures that complement IQ test

sections.heading: CHC Theory: The Map of Human Intelligence
sections.preview: The Cattell-Horn-Carroll (CHC) model is the most empirically supported and widely used theory of cognitive abilities. It integrates: Cattell & Horn's Gf-Gc theory : Fluid intelligence (Gf) vs. Crystallized intelligence (Gc) Carroll's Three-Stratum theory : Hierarchical model from narrow to broad to general Three Strata

sections.heading: The Wechsler Scales
sections.preview: David Wechsler's intelligence scales are the most widely used individually administered intelligence tests in the world . Three versions cover the lifespan: Test Ages Current Edition Core Subtests WPPSI (Preschool/Primary) 2:6 – 7:7 WPPSI-IV Varies by age band WISC (Children) 6:0 – 16:11 WISC-V 7 core for FSIQ WAIS (Ad

sections.heading: Other Major Intelligence Tests

sections.heading: Cognitive Screening Tools
sections.preview: Screening tools are brief instruments used to flag potential cognitive impairment. They are NOT diagnostic — they identify who needs further evaluation. Tool Time Domains Assessed Cutoff Best For MMSE (Mini-Mental State Exam) 5–10 min Orientation, registration, attention/calculation, recall, language ≤23 = impairment M

sections.heading: Adaptive Behavior Assessment
sections.preview: Adaptive behavior refers to the conceptual, social, and practical skills needed for daily functioning. It is required alongside IQ testing for diagnosing intellectual disability (DSM-5). Three domains (AAIDD framework): Conceptual : Language, reading, math, self-direction, memory Social : Interpersonal skills, social r

sections.heading: The Flynn Effect
sections.preview: The Flynn effect (Flynn, 1987) refers to the sustained increase in IQ scores across generations — approximately 3 points per decade (0.3 points per year). Why it matters for clinical practice: A test normed in 2010 will overestimate IQ by ~5 points if used in 2026 (because the comparison group performed worse) This is

sections.heading: Malingering and Effort Testing
sections.preview: Malingering is the intentional production of false or exaggerated symptoms motivated by external incentives (financial gain, avoiding responsibilities, legal advantage). It is NOT a mental disorder — it is a V-code (DSM-5). Distinguishing from related concepts: Concept Intentional? Motivation Malingering Yes External (

sections.heading: Clinical vs. Actuarial Prediction
sections.preview: Paul Meehl's (1954) landmark work established that actuarial (statistical) prediction generally outperforms clinical (subjective) prediction . This remains one of the most robust findings in psychology. Definitions: Clinical prediction : Clinician uses experience, intuition, and subjective judgment to make predictions

sections.heading: Intellectual Disability & Giftedness
sections.preview: Intellectual Disability (DSM-5): Requires all three criteria: Intellectual deficits : Confirmed by clinical assessment and standardized IQ testing (approximately 2+ SD below the mean, ~IQ 65–75 range) Adaptive functioning deficits : In at least one domain (conceptual, social, practical) Onset during the developmental p

sections.heading: Why This Chapter Matters
sections.preview: Personality assessment is one of the most heavily tested topics on the EPPP. You need to know the major objective instruments (MMPI, PAI, NEO-PI-R, MCMI), how projective tests work (Rorschach, TAT), and the ongoing scientific debates about each. This chapter distinguishes between objective (self-report) and projective

sections.heading: Objective vs. Projective Tests
sections.preview: Two fundamental approaches to personality measurement: Feature Objective (Self-Report) Projective Format Structured items (T/F, Likert) Ambiguous stimuli requiring open-ended response Scoring Standardized, mechanical Requires clinical judgment (though R-PAS is more standardized) Reliability Generally high Variable; dep

sections.heading: The MMPI Family: MMPI-2, MMPI-2-RF & MMPI-3
sections.preview: The Minnesota Multiphasic Personality Inventory is the most widely used and researched objective personality test in the world. Know the differences between versions: Feature MMPI-2 (1989) MMPI-2-RF (2008) MMPI-3 (2020) Items 567 338 335 Scale structure 10 Clinical Scales + validity RC Scales + H-O + SP + PSY-5 RC Scal

Scale Name (MMPI-3) What It Detects High Score Means CNS Cannot Say Unanswered items Too many items left blank; profile may be invalid VRIN-r Variable Response Inconsistency Random responding A

sections.heading: Personality Assessment Inventory (PAI)
sections.preview: The PAI (Morey, 1991, 2007) is a 344-item self-report measure for clinical diagnosis and treatment planning. It is an increasingly popular alternative to the MMPI in clinical and forensic settings. Key features: 4th-grade reading level (lower than MMPI's 6th grade) 50–60 minutes to administer Non-overlapping scales : U

sections.heading: NEO-PI-R and the Five-Factor Model
sections.preview: The NEO-PI-R (Costa & McCrae, 1992) is the primary instrument for measuring the Five-Factor Model (FFM) of personality — also known as the "Big Five." The Big Five (OCEAN): Factor High End Low End Facets (6 each) O — Openness Curious, imaginative, creative Conventional, practical Fantasy, Aesthetics, Feelings, Actions,

sections.heading: MCMI-IV (Millon Clinical Multiaxial Inventory)
sections.preview: The MCMI-IV (Millon, 2015) is specifically designed to assess personality disorders and clinical syndromes as defined by DSM-5. It is grounded in Theodore Millon's evolutionary theory of personality. Key features: 195 true/false items (∼25–30 minutes) 5th-grade reading level Only for clinical populations — not normed f

sections.heading: The Rorschach Inkblot Test
sections.preview: The Rorschach consists of 10 inkblot cards (5 achromatic, 2 red-black, 3 multicolored). The test-taker describes what each card might be, and responses are coded for content, determinants, location, and other variables. Scoring Systems: System Developer Status Key Feature Comprehensive System (CS) John Exner Legacy (po

sections.heading: Thematic Apperception Test (TAT)
sections.preview: The TAT (Murray, 1943) presents ambiguous pictures depicting interpersonal scenes. The test-taker creates a story about each picture, including what happened before, what is happening now, what characters are thinking and feeling, and what the outcome will be. Based on the apperception hypothesis: People project their

sections.heading: Why This Chapter Matters
sections.preview: Behavioral assessment asks a fundamentally different question than personality or cognitive testing. Instead of asking "what does this person have?" it asks "what does this person do, and why?" The EPPP tests your knowledge of functional behavior assessment, observation methods, and the behavioral approach to understan

sections.heading: Behavioral vs. Traditional Assessment
sections.preview: Two paradigms: Feature Traditional Assessment Behavioral Assessment Focus Internal traits, dispositions, diagnoses Observable behavior in context Goal Classify the person Understand the behavior’s function Unit of analysis The person (trait = stable across situations) The behavior-environment interaction Role of enviro

sections.heading: ABC Recording: Antecedent-Behavior-Consequence
sections.preview: The ABC model is the fundamental unit of behavioral analysis . It captures the three-term contingency that explains why a behavior occurs: Component Definition Example A — Antecedent What happened immediately before the behavior Teacher gives math assignment B — Behavior The observable action (defined operationally) St
sections.diagramDescription: The ABC Three-Term Contingency Model

sections.heading: Functional Behavior Assessment (FBA)
sections.preview: An FBA is a systematic process for identifying the function of a problem behavior. It uses indirect and descriptive methods (not experimental manipulation). Steps: Define the target behavior in observable, measurable terms (operational definition) Collect data via interviews, rating scales, ABC recording, and direct ob

sections.heading: Functional Analysis (FA)
sections.preview: A Functional Analysis (Iwata et al., 1982/1994) is the experimental method for determining behavioral function. Unlike FBA (which is descriptive), FA involves systematic manipulation of antecedents and consequences to observe their causal effect on behavior. Iwata’s four standard conditions: Condition What Is Manipulat

sections.heading: Behavioral Observation Methods
sections.preview: Behavioral observation involves systematically recording behavior as it occurs . The method you choose depends on the nature of the behavior. Method How It Works Best For Bias Event Recording (Frequency) Count each occurrence of the behavior Discrete behaviors with clear start/end (hand-raising, hitting) Accurate for l

sections.heading: Self-Monitoring and Ecological Momentary Assessment
sections.preview: Self-monitoring involves having the individual systematically observe and record their own behavior. It is both an assessment tool and a reactive intervention — the act of monitoring itself often changes the behavior. Reactivity of self-monitoring: Desirable behaviors tend to increase when monitored Undesirable behavio

sections.heading: The Behavioral Interview
sections.preview: A behavioral interview is a structured or semi-structured interview designed to identify the specific behaviors of concern, their antecedents and consequences, and their environmental context. Key questions in a behavioral interview: What specific behaviors are of concern? (operational definition) When and where do the

sections.heading: Psychophysiological Assessment
sections.preview: Psychophysiological assessment measures bodily responses to psychological states. These are especially important for anxiety, stress, and trauma assessment. Measure What It Assesses Clinical Use Electromyography (EMG) Muscle tension Tension headaches, TMJ, general stress Electrodermal Activity (EDA/GSR) Skin conductanc

sections.heading: Cognitive-Behavioral Assessment
sections.preview: Cognitive-behavioral assessment integrates behavioral observation with the assessment of cognitive processes (thoughts, beliefs, attributions, schemas) that mediate behavior. Key instruments: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI-II) : 21 items assessing cognitive, affective, and somatic symptoms of depression; widely used, w

sections.preview: Clinical interviewing is widely used, but format and scope differ across clinical, forensic, school, health, research, and consultation roles. sections.heading: The Mental Status Exam (MSE)
sections.preview: The MSE is a systematic observation of the patient’s current mental functioning at the time of the interview. sections.heading: Why This Chapter Matters

sections.diagramDescription: Decision path: preserve records and deadlines, identify the demand, verify authority and jurisdiction, consult the client and counsel as appropriate, assert privilege or seek protection when available, and disclose only through a legally authorized route and only within its scope.

sections.heading: Why This Chapter Matters
sections.preview: Assessment ethics bridges the Assessment & Diagnosis domain with Ethics . chapters.title: Research Ethics & the Belmont Report

sections.heading: Why This Chapter Matters
sections.preview: The EPPP tests research ethics separately from clinical ethics. These are recurring exam-content disti

sections.heading: The Belmont Report (1979)
sections.preview: The Belmont Report is the foundational document for ethical research with human subjects. It was developed by the National Commission for the Protection of Human Subjects following ethical scandals (notably the Tuskegee Syphilis Study). Three core principles: Principle Definition Application Respect for Persons Individ
sections.diagramDescription: Three-column concept map connecting Respect for Persons to informed and voluntary consent, Beneficence to risk-benefit assessment, and Justice to equitable subject selection. Each column ends with a protocol-review question.

sections.heading: Institutional Review Boards (IRBs)

Coverage depends on the activity, funding or regulatory authority, institutional assurances, and other applicable rules; investigators sho

sections.preview: Standard 8 of the APA Ethics Code covers research-specific ethical obligations: Standard Topic Key Rule 8.01 IRB Approval Must obtain institutional approval and comply with approved protocol 8.02 Informed Consent to Research Inform participants about: purpose, procedures, right to decline/withdraw, risks and benefits,

sections.heading: Publication Ethics and Scientific Integrity
sections.preview: The APA Ethics Code and broader scientific community require honesty and integrity in publication: Key rules: Plagiarism (Standard 8.11) : Do not present others’ work or data as your own, even in small portions Publication credit (Standard 8.12) : Credit must reflect scientific or professional contributions, not status

No informed consent. Led directly to the Belmont

chapters.title: Psychodynamic & Humanistic Therapies
chapters.reviewNote: Separated historical psychoanalytic theory from established development; corrected Electra attribution, deterministic fixation, transference/countertransference and resistance shortcuts, Mahler’s discarded normal-autistic-phase claim and causal BPD link, diagnosis-by-splitting, the Kohut-versus-Kernberg empathy/confrontation caricature, Rogers’s six versus three conditions, existential absolutes, and technique-only Gestalt framing; added an accessible Rogers pathway diagram and application check and replaced speculative AI consciousness claims with theory/evidence/context safeguards.

sections.heading: Why This Chapter Matters
sections.preview: The EPPP tests your ability to match therapeutic techniques to their theoretical origins. You must distinguish between psychoanalytic/psychodynamic approaches (insight, unconscious, transference) and humanistic/existential approaches (self-actualization, present experience, meaning). This chapter covers Freud through m

sections.heading: Classical Psychoanalysis (Freud)
sections.preview: Sigmund Freud developed psychoanalysis. The concepts below are historically and theoretically important; they are not all empirically established descriptions of development or mechanisms. Concept Careful exam-ready description Structural model Freud proposed id, ego, and superego as interacting theoretical agencies—no
sections.diagramDescription: 🧊 Freud's Structural Iceberg Model. The Ego represents conscious reality, the Superego spans all levels to represent morality, and the Id operates completely unconsciously on the pleasure principle. Water level indicates the threshold of consciousness.

sections.heading: Object Relations Theory
sections.preview: Object relations theories shifted psychoanalytic attention from drives toward relationships and internal representations of self and others. Here, “object” is historical psychoanalytic terminology for a significant person or representation. Theorist Theoretical contribution Important qualification Melanie Klein Paranoi

sections.heading: Self Psychology (Kohut)
sections.preview: Heinz Kohut developed self psychology, a psychoanalytic theory emphasizing cohesion of the self and lifelong selfobject functions. Selfobject: a person or experience functioning in support of self-cohesion. Mirroring, idealizing, and twinship: commonly taught selfobject needs involving affirmation, connection to valued

sections.heading: Person-Centered Therapy (Rogers)
sections.preview: Carl Rogers developed person-centered therapy, emphasizing the client's frame of reference and an actualizing tendency under facilitative relational conditions. Three commonly taught therapist attitudes: Attitude Description Congruence The therapist is genuine within appropriate professional boundaries; congruence is n
sections.diagramDescription: Rogers’s six-condition relational pathway

sections.heading: Existential Therapy
sections.preview: Existential therapies explore how people relate to givens of existence, choice, responsibility, finitude, connection, and meaning. They form a diverse family of philosophical-clinical approaches rather than one standardized protocol. Yalom's concern Clinical theme Death Awareness of mortality may evoke anxiety and can

sections.heading: Gestalt Therapy (Perls)
sections.preview: Gestalt therapy , associated with Fritz and Laura Perls and Paul Goodman, emphasizes awareness, contact, embodiment, context, and present-centered exploration. Contemporary Gestalt practice is relational and should not be reduced to a collection of dramatic techniques. Here-and-now: explore how past or anticipated expe

chapters.title: Cognitive & Behavioral Therapies
chapters.reviewNote: Corrected one-way cognitive causation, Beck-versus-Ellis style caricatures, DBT-equals-BPD and ACT-versus-CBT binaries, exposure-for-all-anxiety and habituation-only accounts, flooding/PE conflation, fixed gold-standard and comparative-efficacy claims, relaxation prohibition, guaranteed extinction bursts, and anthropomorphic AI therapy analogies; added a labelled exposure-learning cycle and an ERP learning-outcome check.

sections.heading: Why This Chapter Matters
sections.preview: Cognitive and behavioral therapies are prominent in psychotherapy research and EPPP treatment questions. Learn the core models (Beck, Ellis), contextual or “third-wave” approaches (DBT, ACT), and behavioral procedures such as exposure and contingency management. For vignettes, identify the intervention from its definin

sections.heading: Beck’s Cognitive Therapy
sections.preview: Aaron Beck developed cognitive therapy, an important foundation for modern CBT. Cognitive formulations propose reciprocal links among situations, appraisals, emotions, physiology, and behavior. Biased or unhelpful appraisals can maintain distress, but they are not the sole cause of psychological problems, and CBT does
sections.diagramDescription: Beck's Cognitive Model of Depression

sections.heading: Rational Emotive Behavior Therapy (Ellis)
sections.preview: Albert Ellis developed REBT, an action-oriented therapy that focuses on identifying and disputing irrational beliefs . The ABC(DE) Model: Letter Component Example A Activating Event You fail an exam B Belief (rational or irrational) "I MUST succeed at everything I attempt" (irrational) C Consequence (emotional/behavior
sections.diagramDescription: 🔺 Cognitive-Behavioral Therapy holds that psychological distress is maintained by the reciprocal interactions between core beliefs, automatic thoughts, emotional states, and behavioral responses. Intervening at any corner of the triangle impacts the others.

sections.heading: Dialectical Behavior Therapy (Linehan)
sections.preview: Marsha Linehan developed DBT initially for people with pervasive emotion dysregulation and recurrent suicidal or self-harming behavior, and it became especially associated with borderline personality disorder (BPD). Standard comprehensive DBT integrates behavioral analysis and change procedures with validation, accepta
sections.diagramDescription: ☯️ Dialectical Behavior Therapy (DBT) relies heavily on four core skills modules that balance the central dialectic of Acceptance (Mindfulness & Distress Tolerance) and Change (Emotion Regulation & Interpersonal Effectiveness).

sections.heading: Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (Hayes)
sections.preview: Steven Hayes developed ACT (pronounced as one word, "act"), a "third-wave" behavioral therapy rooted in Relational Frame Theory (RFT) . Core premise: Suffering comes not from pain itself but from psychological inflexibility — the rigid dominance of psychological reactions over chosen values and actions. The Hexaflex —

sections.heading: Behavioral Techniques: Exposure Therapies
sections.preview: Exposure-based interventions draw on conditioning, emotional-processing, and inhibitory-learning accounts. They involve planned contact with feared but sufficiently safe cues while reducing avoidance or safety behavior so new learning can occur. Exposure is a component of several evidence-based protocols, but “the trea
sections.diagramDescription: Exposure learning cycle: prediction, safe approach, response prevention, and new learning

sections.heading: Behavioral Techniques: Operant Approaches
sections.preview: Operant-based interventions use consequences to modify behavior. They are grounded in Skinner’s principles. Technique Description Application Behavioral Activation (BA) Functional assessment and planned engagement with reinforcing or valued activities while reducing avoidance Depression . An evidence-based treatment; c
sections.diagramDescription: 🐀 The Four Quadrants of Operant Conditioning. Remember: Positive means ADDING something, Negative means REMOVING something. Reinforcement INCREASES behavior, Punishment DECREASES behavior. The flashing green box highlights Negative Reinforcement (removing a bad thing to increase a behavior), which is highly tested and often confused with punishment.

chapters.title: Family, Couples & Group Therapy
chapters.reviewNote: Corrected structural-family causation and universal hierarchy/boundary norms, unsafe strategic and ordeal shortcuts, Bowenian blame and birth-order/transmission determinism, Gottman divorce-prediction and contempt-ranking certainty, EFT superiority, fixed Yalom factor rankings, and individual-only narrative framing; rebuilt the boundary diagram as a labelled contextual theory continuum, added a non-blaming systems-formulation check, revised the Gottman check to identify behavior rather than predict divorce, and replaced AI-as-family-member speculation with privacy, consent, coercion, and role safeguards.

sections.heading: Why This Chapter Matters

sections.heading: Structural Family Therapy (Minuchin)

The model does not establish that family structure is the sole cause of a member’s problem. Key concepts: Family structure : The invisible rules that

Eight key concepts: Concept Definition Differentiation of Self A Bowenian construct describing the capacity to maintain thoughtful, values-consistent functioning while remaining emo

sections.preview: John Gottman developed a research-based approach to couples therapy built on decades of observational research at his "Love Lab." sections.preview: Sue Johnson developed Emotionally Focused Therapy (EFT) for couples, grounded in attachment theory . sections.heading: Narrative Therapy (White & Epston)

Core concepts: The person is not the problem; the problem is the problem — externaliza

chapters.title: Evidence-Based Practice & Psychopharmacology
chapters.reviewNote: Corrected EBPP component misuse, EST reasoning, Dodo-bird and Lambert percentage overclaims, alliance causality, fixed dose-response/deterioration estimates, antidepressant response and warning language, and psychotropic class absolutes; updated clozapine REMS, stimulant, buspirone, lithium, and lamotrigine guidance; rebuilt the common-factors check and grounded AI evaluation in EBPP.

sections.heading: Why This Chapter Matters
sections.preview: The EPPP tests whether you understand the current model of evidence-based practice, the debate over what makes therapy work (specific factors vs. common factors), and the major classes of psychotropic medications. Prescribing authority is jurisdiction- and credential-specific. Psychologists should recognize major medic

sections.heading: Evidence-Based Practice in Psychology (EBPP)

Different disorders require different specific treatments

sections.diagramDescription: 💊 Synaptic Transmission & Drug Mechanisms. SSRIs (like Prozac) block the reuptake pump, keeping more serotonin in the synapse. MAOIs block the monoamine oxidase (MAO) enzyme from destroying neurotransmitters. Both effectively increase neurotransmitter activity.

chapters.title: Prevention & Community Psychology

sections.preview: The EPPP explicitly includes prevention in this domain title. The systems answer different questions, so the same program c

sections.heading: Community Psychology Principles
sections.preview: Community psychology shifts the focus from individual pathology to contextual and systemic factors. chapters.title: Crisis Intervention & Trauma Treatment

sections.preview: Crisis and trauma questions require prioritized, context-sensitive reasoning about suicide risk assessment (risk/protective factors, safety planning), crisis intervention models , and the evidence-based treatments for PTSD (Prolonged Exposure, CPT, EMDR). The exam will present crisis scenarios and ask you to identify t

sections.preview: Crisis : A time-limited period of psychological disequilibrium triggered by an event that overwhelms the individual’s usual coping mechanisms. chapters.title: Stages of Change, MI & Special Populations
chapters.reviewNote: Corrected TTM resistance labels and guaranteed stage matching, relapse inevitability, MI intrinsic-motivation and sustain-talk/discord conflation, rigid multicultural first-step rules, play-therapy-by-age and child verbal-capacity claims, underspecified adolescent confidentiality, older-adult stereotypes, MCI-to-dementia implication, and reversible-pseudodementia shortcut; rebuilt and labelled the TTM diagram with non-stigmatizing return/reassessment paths, added a later-life cognitive differential check, updated the AI coda for autonomy and privacy, and grounded developmental adaptations in individualized assessment.

sections.heading: Why This Chapter Matters
sections.preview: The EPPP tests the Transtheoretical Model (TTM), Motivational Interviewing (MI), multicultural responsiveness, and developmentally appropriate care. Learn model-specific vocabulary while avoiding age, culture, or stage labels as substitutes for individualized assessment, consent, context, evidence, and client goals.

sections.heading: The Transtheoretical Model (Prochaska & DiClemente)
sections.preview: The Transtheoretical Model (TTM) describes how people change behavior, whether or not they are in therapy. Change is a process , not an event. Stage Client Characteristics Therapeutic Strategy Precontemplation Not currently considering change, which may reflect different priorities, limited information, low perceived r
sections.diagramDescription: TTM stages as a revisable change snapshot

sections.heading: Motivational Interviewing (Miller & Rollnick)
sections.preview: Motivational Interviewing (MI) is a collaborative, person-centered way of communicating about change and growth that pays particular attention to the person’s own language, reasons, autonomy, and commitment. It can explore ambivalence without assuming every person is ambivalent or that motivation is purely “intrinsic.”

sections.heading: Multicultural Competence (Sue)
sections.preview: Derald Wing Sue and colleagues advanced a widely taught tripartite framework of awareness/beliefs, knowledge, and skills. It is foundational exam vocabulary but not a complete endpoint for culturally responsive practice, which also involves humility, power, structural context, language, intersectionality, and ongoing a

sections.heading: Treatment of Children & Adolescents
sections.preview: Working with children requires developmentally appropriate modifications to standard therapeutic approaches. The therapi

sections.heading: Treatment of Older Adults (Geropsychology)
sections.preview: Geropsychology addresses the unique mental health needs of older adults. Key considerations: Diagnostic complexity : Cognitive complaints require assessment of onset, course, function, mood, delirium, medications, sensory access, sleep, medical/metabolic causes, substance use, and neurocognitive disorders. Avoid using

chapters.title: Neuroanatomy & Brain Function
chapters.reviewNote: Corrected one-symptom/one-lesion reasoning, fixed-voltage neuron absolutes, lobe-to-deficit certainty, intact-comprehension and absent-awareness aphasia claims, arcuate-only conduction aphasia, hippocampal short-to-long conversion, amygdala fear-center and preconscious-detector claims, medulla-damage-equals-death, simplified H.M. anatomy and memory conclusions, split-brain independence, and logical-left/holistic-right myths; rebuilt and labelled the language diagram with a network caveat, added a lateralization myth check, and replaced AI-brain analogies with network-first localization safeguards.

sections.heading: Why This Chapter Matters
sections.preview: The EPPP tests high-yield associations between neural systems and behavior. Use them as localization hypotheses, not one-symptom/one-lesion rules: real deficits depend on lesion location and extent, connected networks, timing, premorbid function, task demands, and recovery.

sections.heading: The Neuron & Neural Communication
sections.preview: The neuron is the basic unit of the nervous system. Structure: Dendrites : Receive incoming signals from other neurons Cell body (soma) : Contains the nucleus; integrates incoming signals Axon : Transmits electrical impulse (action potential) away from the cell body Myelin sheath : Fatty insulation that speeds conducti
sections.diagramDescription: 🔬 A neuron showing dendrites (receive), soma (integrate), axon with myelin sheaths (conduct), and terminal buttons (release neurotransmitters). The green pulse shows the action potential traveling along the axon.

sections.heading: Cerebral Cortex: The Four Lobes
sections.preview: The four-lobe scheme is a useful anatomical organizer. Functions emerge from distributed cortical–subcortical networks, so the table lists common associations rather than exclusive modules or certain lesion outcomes: Lobe Location Key Functions Damage Produces Frontal Front of brain Executive functions (planning, judgm
sections.diagramDescription: 🧩 The four major brain lobes: Frontal (blue — executive function), Parietal (purple — sensory/spatial), Temporal (yellow — memory/language), Occipital (green — vision). Broca's and Wernicke's areas are highlighted with pulsing circles.

sections.heading: Language Areas: Broca’s and Wernicke’s
sections.preview: The classic Broca–Wernicke model remains common exam vocabulary, but contemporary language neuroscience describes distributed dorsal and ventral networks with substantial individual variation: Area Location Function Aphasia Type Characteristics Broca’s area Left frontal lobe (inferior frontal gyrus) Classically associa
sections.diagramDescription: Classic language model and modern network qualification

sections.heading: Subcortical Structures
sections.preview: Structure Location Key Functions Clinical Significance Thalamus Central brain Major set of nuclei that relays and modulates sensory, motor, limbic, cognitive, arousal, and sleep-related information. Each major sensory system except the initial olfactory pathway has a thalamic relay before primary cortex. Damage: sensor

sections.heading: Lateralization & Split-Brain Research
sections.preview: The two cerebral hemispheres are connected by the corpus callosum . While most functions are bilateral, some are lateralized: Left Hemisphere Right Hemisphere Language is left-lateralized in most people, with task and individual variation Some visuospatial attention and face-processing functions show rightward speciali
sections.diagramDescription: 👁️ The Visual Pathway. Note the contralateral processing: The entirely LEFT Visual Field (Blue) hits the nasal retina of the left eye and temporal retina of the right eye, crossing at the Optic Chiasm to be processed in the RIGHT Occipital Lobe. Conversely, the entirely RIGHT Visual Field (Red) is processed in the LEFT Occipital Lobe. Damage before the chiasm affects one eye; damage after the chiasm affects one entire visual field.

chapters.title: Neurotransmitters, Endocrine System & Genetics
chapters.reviewNote: Corrected one-neurotransmitter/one-disorder and too-low/too-high tables, global dopamine schizophrenia claims, pituitary hormone-origin errors, categorical cortisol/hippocampus injury and universal thyroid-testing rules, fixed twin/concordance/IQ estimates, deterministic adoption inference, contested 5-HTTLPR example, simplistic epigenetic on/off and human transgenerational inheritance, REM-beta and fixed-cycle claims, and absolute parasomnia distinctions; rebuilt and labelled the dopamine diagram, added a heritability interpretation check, and replaced substrate analogies with mechanism-association-inference safeguards.

sections.heading: Why This Chapter Matters
sections.preview: The EPPP tests neurotransmitter systems, endocrine stress regulation, behavioral-genetic concepts, and sleep physiology. Learn pathway-level associations while avoiding “one chemical causes one disorder,” genetic determinism, or fixed biological values detached from population and context.

sections.heading: Major Neurotransmitters
sections.preview: Neurotransmitters act through multiple receptor subtypes and circuits. sections.heading: Why This Chapter Matters

sections.heading: Atkinson-Shiffrin Model (Multi-Store Model)
sections.preview: The Atkinson-Shiffrin (1968) model describes three distinct memory stores: Store Capacity Duration Key Features Sensory memory Very large <1 second (iconic/visual) to ~3–5 seconds (echoic/auditory) Iconic (Sperling) = visual; Echoic = auditory. Requires attention to transfer to STM. Short-term memory (STM) Historica
sections.diagramDescription: 🧠 The Atkinson-Shiffrin 3-Stage Memory Model. Sensory memory briefly holds exact environmental info. Paying ATTENTION moves it to Short-Term Memory (STM) where it loops via rehearsal. ENCODING moves it to permanent Long-Term Memory (LTM), and RETRIEVAL brings it back to STM for use.

sections.heading: Working Memory (Baddeley)

sections.heading: Forgetting & Memory Distortion

chapters.title: Cognition, Problem-Solving & Decision-Making

sections.heading: Why This Chapter Matters
sections.preview: The EPPP tests your knowledge of cognitive biases and heuristics (Kahneman & Tversky), dual-process theory , and problem-solving strategies . These concepts apply to clinical judgment (how clinicians make diagnostic errors), forensic psychology (eyewitness decisions), and everyday reasoning.

sections.heading: Heuristics & Cognitive Biases
sections.preview: Kahneman & Tversky identified mental shortcuts (heuristics) that are efficient but can lead to systematic errors (biases): Heuristic/Bias Definition Example Availability heuristic Judging likelihood based on how easily examples come to mind After seeing news about plane crashes, overestimating the risk of flying (even

sections.heading: Dual-Process Theory (System 1 & System 2)
sections.preview: Kahneman’s (2011) dual-process theory describes two modes of thinking: System 1 System 2 Fast, automatic, intuitive Slow, deliberate, analytical Often rapid and relatively automatic Effortful, requires concentration Prone to biases and heuristics Can override System 1 (but requires effort) Pattern recognition, emotiona
sections.diagramDescription: Dual-process reasoning as interacting modes with a structured cross-check rather than two literal brain modules

sections.heading: Problem-Solving Strategies
sections.preview: Problem-solving approaches: Algorithms : Defined step-by-step procedures that yield a solution when the procedure is valid, applicable, and correctly executed; feasibility and efficiency vary Heuristics : Mental shortcuts that are faster but not guaranteed (efficient but error-prone) Insight : Sudden "aha!" realization

chapters.title: Emotion & Motivation

sections.heading: Why This Chapter Matters
sections.preview: The EPPP tests your understanding of how emotions arise (competing theories), what motivates behavior, and how these concepts apply to clinical practice. You must distinguish between the major emotion theories and know the key motivation frameworks.

sections.heading: Theories of Emotion

sections.heading: Why This Chapter Matters
sections.preview: Executive function and self-regulation are tested across several EPPP domains — from neuropsych (frontal lobe damage) to development (EF in children) to treatment (CBT relies on metacognition). This chapter integrates these concepts and introduces the foundational theories you need to know.

sections.heading: Executive Functions

Key theories: Baumeister’s ego depletion model : Self-control is a limited resource (like a muscle) that becomes depleted with use. After exerting self-control, subsequent self-control tasks are harder. Controversial

sections.heading: Attention

sections.heading: Attribution Theory

sections.heading: Why This Chapter Matters

sections.heading: Conformity
sections.preview: Conformity = adjusting behavior or beliefs to match group norms. Asch’s line study (1951): Participants compared line lengths with confederates who unanimously gave wrong answers ~75% of participants conformed at least once; ~33% conformed on average Conformity decreased significantly when even one ally dissented Confo
sections.diagramDescription: 👥 Asch Conformity Experiment. Demonstrated the power of normative social influence. Participants conformed to the clearly incorrect majority answer on about 32% of trials. Conformity dropped sharply if even ONE confederate gave the correct answer (breaking unanimity) or if the participant could write their answer privately.

For easy tasks, the dominant response is c

sections.heading: Why This Chapter Matters
sections.preview: This chapter covers attitude formation and change, persuasion models, and the psychology of prejudice and discrimination. These concepts are essential for understanding therapeutic change, public health messaging, and multicultural competence.

sections.heading: Attitudes & Attitude Change
sections.preview: Attitudes have three components (the ABC model ): Affective : Emotional response ("I feel anxious about public speaking") Behavioral : Actions toward the object ("I avoid public speaking") Cognitive : Beliefs about the object ("Public speaking is dangerous") Attitude change theories: Cognitive dissonance (Festinger) :
sections.diagramDescription: ⚡ Festinger's Cognitive Dissonance Theory. When our attitudes and behaviors clash, we experience an uncomfortable tension (dissonance). Because changing behavior (quitting smoking) is often difficult, people frequently resolve the tension by changing their attitude or rationalizing the behavior (e.g., adding a new belief like "smoking lowers my stress").

sections.heading: Why This Chapter Matters
sections.preview: The EPPP tests theories of helping behavior, aggression, and interpersonal attraction. These concepts connect to clinical work (aggression in conduct disorder, attachment in relationships), forensic psychology, and developmental psychology.

sections.heading: Prosocial Behavior & Altruism

sections.heading: Why This Chapter Matters
sections.preview: Piaget and Vygotsky are among the most tested theorists on the entire EPPP. You need to know each of Piaget’s stages, key concepts, and limitations, as well as Vygotsky’s Zone of Proximal Development and how it contrasts with Piaget.

sections.preview: Theory of Mind (ToM): Understanding that others have different beliefs, desires, and knowledge from your own. Develops around age 4–5 Tested with false-belief tasks (Sally-Anne test): Can the child understand that Sally will look where she left the marble, not where Anne moved it? sections.heading: Why This Chapter Matters
sections.preview: Erikson’s identity vs. role confusion, Marcia’s identity statuses, and Kohlberg’s moral development are heavily tested. This chapter also covers adolescent brain development, which explains risk-taking behavior.

sections.heading: Erikson’s Psychosocial Theory (Review)
sections.preview: Erik Erikson (1963) proposed 8 stages of psychosocial development, each defined by a crisis. All 8 stages are testable, but adolescence and adulthood stages appear most frequently. Stage Age Crisis Key Outcome 1 0–1 Trust vs. Mistrust Hope — responsive caregiver → trust 2 1–3 Autonomy vs. Shame/Doubt Will — encourageme
sections.diagramDescription: 📊 Erikson's 8 psychosocial stages from birth through old age, showing the core crisis and associated virtue at each stage. The green dot traces the lifespan journey.

sections.heading: Marcia’s Identity Statuses
sections.preview: James Marcia (1966) expanded Erikson’s identity concept into four statuses based on two dimensions: exploration (crisis) and commitment : Commitment present No commitment Exploration occurred Identity Achievement — explored and committed (best outcomes) Moratorium — actively exploring but no commitment yet No explorati

sections.heading: Moral Development
sections.preview: Kohlberg’s stages of moral reasoning: Level Stage Orientation Preconventional (most children, some adults) Stage 1 Obedience/Punishment : "I’ll get punished" Stage 2 Self-Interest : "What’s in it for me?" Conventional (most adolescents/adults) Stage 3 Good Boy/Good Girl : "Others will approve" Stage 4 Law & Order : "It
sections.diagramDescription: ⚖️ Kohlberg's Stages of Moral Development. Focuses on the reasoning behind moral choices (like the Heinz Dilemma), not just the choice itself. Moves from avoiding punishment (Preconventional) to following society's rules (Conventional) to personal, universal ethics (Postconventional). Carol Gilligan famously criticized Kohlberg for neglecting the "ethics of care" more prevalent in women.

sections.heading: Adolescent Brain Development & Risk-Taking
sections.preview: Why do adolescents take risks? The answer is differential brain maturation : The limbic system (emotional/reward processing, amygdala, nucleus accumbens) matures early in adolescence The prefrontal cortex (impulse control, planning, judgment) doesn’t fully mature until the mid-20s This creates a mismatch : strong rewar

sections.heading: Why This Chapter Matters
sections.preview: The EPPP tests cognitive aging, dementia types, grief models, and successful aging theories. You must distinguish normal age-related cognitive changes from pathological decline and know multiple grief/end-of-life models.

sections.heading: Cognitive Aging
sections.preview: Normal aging: Declines With Age Stable or Increases With Age Fluid intelligence (novel problem-solving, processing speed) Crystallized intelligence (vocabulary, accumulated knowledge) Working memory capacity Semantic memory (word meanings, general facts) Processing speed Procedural memory (how to ride a bike) Divided a

sections.heading: Theories of Successful Aging
sections.preview: Three major theories: Theory Core Claim Evaluation Disengagement theory (Cumming & Henry) Aging involves mutual withdrawal between the individual and society. This is natural and healthy. Largely discredited . Disengagement is not beneficial for most older adults. Activity theory (Havighurst) Successful aging = staying

sections.heading: Grief & End of Life
sections.preview: Kübler-Ross (1969) — Five stages of grief: Denial : "This can’t be happening" Anger : "Why me?" Bargaining : "If only I had..." Depression : Sadness and withdrawal as reality sets in Acceptance : Coming to terms with the loss Important: These stages are not necessarily sequential , not everyone experiences all stages,

sections.heading: Why This Chapter Matters
sections.preview: Research design is foundational for evaluating clinical evidence. The EPPP tests your ability to distinguish experimental from correlational designs, identify appropriate statistical tests, and evaluate the quality of research conclusions.

sections.heading: Types of Research Designs
sections.preview: Design Key Feature Can Establish Causation? True Experimental Random assignment to conditions + manipulation of IV Yes Quasi-Experimental Manipulation of IV but no random assignment Limited (preexisting group differences) Correlational Measures relationship between variables; no manipulation No (correlation ≠ causation

sections.heading: Between-Subjects, Within-Subjects & Factorial Designs
sections.preview: Between-subjects (independent groups): Different participants in each condition Requires more participants No order/carryover effects Individual differences can confound results Within-subjects (repeated measures): Same participants in all conditions More powerful (controls for individual differences) Vulnerable to ord

sections.heading: Single-Subject Designs
sections.preview: Used in clinical and behavioral research when group designs are impractical: Design Structure Key Features ABA (Reversal) Baseline → Treatment → Baseline If behavior returns to baseline, IV likely caused change. Not ethical when withdrawal is harmful. ABAB Baseline → Treatment → Baseline → Treatment Stronger than ABA.

sections.heading: Why This Chapter Matters
sections.preview: Threats to validity are among the most directly tested topics in research methods. The EPPP presents research scenarios and asks you to identify the specific threat. You must know each threat by name, definition, and example.

sections.heading: Internal Validity Threats
sections.preview: Internal validity = confidence that the IV caused the change in the DV. Threats are alternative explanations: Threat Definition Example History External events occurring during the study A national crisis during a stress-reduction study Maturation Natural changes in participants over time Children improving on a readin

sections.heading: External Validity Threats
sections.preview: External validity = generalizability of results to other populations, settings, and times. Threat Definition Population validity Results may not generalize to other populations (e.g., WEIRD samples) Ecological validity Lab findings may not apply to real-world settings Temporal validity Results may not generalize across

sections.heading: Construct & Statistical Conclusion Validity
sections.preview: Construct validity (of the study) = Are we actually measuring/manipulating the constructs we claim? Demand characteristics : Participants guess the hypothesis and change behavior accordingly Experimenter expectancy (Rosenthal effect) : Researcher’s expectations influence participant behavior Social desirability : Parti
sections.diagramDescription: 📊 Construct Validity Framework. Construct validity is the "umbrella" term asking if your measure actually captures the theoretical construct. Content validity asks if you sampled all parts of the subject. Criterion validity relies on external data (Concurrent = NOW, Predictive = FUTURE). Convergent/Discriminant validity ensures your construct correlates with similar traits but NOT with unrelated traits.

sections.heading: Why This Chapter Matters
sections.preview: Statistics is a core component of the EPPP. You won’t be asked to calculate, but you must know which test to use when , interpret results, and understand Type I/II errors, power, and effect sizes.

sections.heading: Descriptive Statistics
sections.preview: Measures of central tendency: Mean : Arithmetic average. Most affected by outliers. Median : Middle score. Best for skewed distributions. Mode : Most frequent score. Only measure for nominal data. Skewness: Positive skew : Tail extends to the right. Mean > Median > Mode. (Most scores are low.) Negative skew : Tail exte

sections.heading: Inferential Statistics: Choosing the Right Test
sections.preview: Decision tree for choosing statistical tests: Situation Test Compare 2 group means (independent) Independent t-test Compare 2 group means (same participants) Paired/Dependent t-test Compare 3+ group means (1 IV) One-way ANOVA Compare means with 2+ IVs (factorial) Factorial ANOVA Same participants across 3+ conditions R

sections.heading: Type I/II Errors, Power & Effect Size
sections.preview: Hypothesis testing errors: H₀ is TRUE (no real effect) H₀ is FALSE (real effect exists) Reject H₀ Type I error (α) — false positive Correct! (Power = 1 – β) Fail to reject H₀ Correct! Type II error (β) — false negative Type I error (α) : Concluding there’s an effect when there isn’t. Controlled by setting α (usually .0

sections.heading: Correlation & Regression
sections.preview: Correlation (Pearson r): Ranges from -1.0 to +1.0 Sign = direction (positive or negative relationship) Absolute value = strength (0 = none, .1 = weak, .3 = moderate, .5 = strong) Correlation ≠ causation (third variable problem, directionality problem) r² (coefficient of determination) : Proportion of variance in Y expl

sections.heading: Why This Chapter Matters
sections.preview: The EPPP includes questions on advanced statistical methods. You don’t need to calculate, but you must know what each method does, when it’s used, and how to interpret results. Program evaluation models also appear.

sections.heading: Nonparametric Tests
sections.preview: Use when: Data violate parametric assumptions (non-normal, ordinal data, small samples). Parametric Test Nonparametric Alternative Independent t-test Mann-Whitney U Paired t-test Wilcoxon signed-rank One-way ANOVA Kruskal-Wallis H Repeated measures ANOVA Friedman Pearson r Spearman rho (ρ) Chi-square (χ²) : Used for ca

sections.heading: Factor Analysis, SEM & Meta-Analysis
sections.preview: Factor analysis: Exploratory (EFA) : Discovers underlying factor structure from data. Used when you don’t know the structure. Common in scale development. Confirmatory (CFA) : Tests whether data fit a pre-specified factor model. Used when you have a theoretical structure to test. Factor loadings : Correlations between

sections.heading: Program Evaluation & Epidemiology

sections.heading: Why This Chapter Matters
sections.preview: Research ethics questions appear on both the Ethics and Research Methods sections of the EPPP. You must know the key ethical documents, IRB requirements, and rules around deception, informed consent, and data management.

sections.heading: Foundational Documents
sections.preview: Belmont Report (1979) — Three core principles: Respect for Persons : Autonomy + protection of vulnerable individuals. Requires informed consent . Beneficence : Maximize benefits, minimize risks. Requires risk-benefit analysis . Justice : Fair distribution of research burdens and benefits. No exploitation of vulnerable

sections.heading: APA Ethics Code Standard 8: Research
sections.preview: Key standards for research: 8.01 Institutional Approval : Obtain IRB approval before beginning research 8.02 Informed Consent : Participants must understand purpose, procedures, risks, benefits, right to withdraw, limits of confidentiality 8.03 Consent for Recording : Get permission before recording voices or images 8.

sections.heading: IRB Process, Data Management & Reproducibility
sections.preview: Institutional Review Board (IRB): Review Level Criteria Exempt Minimal risk: educational settings, surveys, existing data. No ongoing IRB oversight. Expedited Minimal risk with some procedures (blood draws, moderate exercise). One or two IRB members review. Full board Greater than minimal risk. Requires review by full

sections.heading: Why This Chapter Matters
sections.preview: This closing chapter covers foundational topics that cut across research design and statistics: sampling methods, measurement scales, reliability/validity in research context, and special design considerations. These "connecting" concepts tie together everything in the domain.

sections.heading: Sampling Methods
sections.preview: Probability sampling (every member has a known chance of selection — generalizable): Method Description Simple random Every member has an equal chance of selection Stratified random Population divided into strata (e.g., gender, ethnicity), then random sample from each stratum Cluster Randomly select groups (clusters),

sections.heading: Scales of Measurement
sections.preview: Stevens’ four scales of measurement (NOIR): Scale Properties Examples Statistics Nominal Categories only (no order) Gender, diagnosis, race Mode, chi-square Ordinal Ordered categories (unequal intervals) Class rank, Likert items, severity ratings Median, Spearman, nonparametric tests Interval Equal intervals, no true z
sections.diagramDescription: 🎯 The classic Target Analogy for Measurement. Reliability = Consistency (grouping of the hits). Validity = Accuracy (hitting the center bullseye). A test must be reliable before it can be valid.

sections.heading: Special Research Considerations
sections.preview: Cross-sectional vs. Longitudinal vs. Cross-sequential: Design Description Strength Weakness Cross-sectional Different age groups measured at one time Quick, inexpensive Cohort effects (generational differences) Longitudinal Same group measured over time Tracks developmental change Time-consuming, attrition , practice e

sections.heading: Understanding the Beast
sections.preview: The EPPP (Examination for Professional Practice in Psychology) is less a test of your clinical intuition and more a test of your ability to memorize and apply specific theoretical frameworks, research findings, and ethical codes exactly as the ASPPB defines them. Format: 225 multiple-choice questions (only 175 are scor

sections.heading: Core Test-Taking Strategies
sections.preview: Mastering the content is only half the battle. Surviving the exam format requires strict adherence to these strategies: Strategy Application The "Textbook" World Answer questions as if you live in a perfect, well-funded, textbook universe. Do not use your messy practicum experience to inform your answers. If the APA Et

sections.heading: Navigating Ethical Dilemmas
sections.preview: Ethics questions are highly procedural. Follow the ASPPB's implicit hierarchy of actions: Is there an imminent threat to life? (e.g., Tarasoff warning, child abuse reporting). If yes, breach confidentiality immediately. Is there a legal mandate? (e.g., a judge's court order). If yes, comply. (Note: A subpoena is NOT a

sections.heading: Managing Exam Fatigue
sections.preview: Decision fatigue is real. By question 150, your prefrontal cortex will want to quit. To combat this: Pacing: Aim to complete 53 questions every hour. Write "53", "106", "159", "212" on your scratch paper to track targets. The "Brain Dump": During the tutorial period before the timer starts, use your whiteboard/scratch

chapters.title: High-Yield Clinical Psychopharmacology
chapters.reviewNote: Corrected antipsychotic class guarantees, clozapine REMS and neutropenia terminology, antidepressant and MAOI absolutes, serotonin-toxicity pattern, lithium renal/monitoring claims, lamotrigine rash context, benzodiazepine/buspirone absolutes, stimulant growth/tic determinism, and invalid AI-clinical metaphors; added an accessible medication-safety recognition map.

sections.heading: Antipsychotics (Neuroleptics)
sections.preview: First-Generation (Typical) Antipsychotics: Examples: Haloperidol (Haldol), Chlorpromazine (Thorazine). Mechanism: Dopamine (D2) receptor antagonists. Effect: Can reduce psychotic symptoms; efficacy and tolerability vary by medication and patient, and persistent negative or cognitive symptoms require broader assessment.
sections.diagramDescription: A recognition—not prescribing—map linking antipsychotics to movement, metabolic, and severe neutropenia monitoring; serotonergic combinations to serotonin toxicity; lithium to level, renal, thyroid, hydration, and interaction monitoring; lamotrigine to serious-rash vigilance; and sedatives or stimulants to individualized safety monitoring.

sections.heading: Antidepressants
sections.preview: Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors (SSRIs): Examples: Fluoxetine (Prozac), Sertraline (Zoloft), Escitalopram (Lexapro). Common options across several depressive and anxiety-related disorders; selection depends on the specific diagnosis, patient factors, evidence, interactions, risks, and preferences. Side Effects:
sections.diagramDescription: 💊 Synaptic Transmission & Drug Mechanisms. SSRIs (like Prozac) block the reuptake pump, keeping more serotonin in the synapse. MAOIs block the monoamine oxidase (MAO) enzyme from destroying neurotransmitters. Both effectively increase neurotransmitter activity.

sections.heading: Mood Stabilizers
sections.preview: Medication roles differ across acute mania, bipolar depression, and maintenance; indication and patient-specific safety factors matter. Lithium: An established treatment for bipolar disorder with a narrow therapeutic range. Monitoring may include serum concentration, renal and thyroid function, electrolytes, hydration/

sections.heading: Anxiolytics & Stimulants
sections.preview: Benzodiazepines (Anxiolytics): Examples: Alprazolam (Xanax), Diazepam (Valium), Lorazepam (Ativan). Mechanism: Enhance GABA (the brain's primary inhibitory neurotransmitter). Side Effects: Sedation, cognitive/motor impairment, can cause sedation and psychomotor/cognitive impairment and may lead to tolerance, physical d

sections.heading: The Normal Distribution & Standard Scores
sections.preview: Standardizing scores allows us to compare results across entirely different tests. All standard scores (z-scores, T-scores, IQ scores) are based on the standard normal distribution, where 68% of scores fall within 1 SD of the mean, 95% within 2 SDs, and 99.7% within 3 SDs.
sections.diagramDescription: The Normal Distribution: Mean (μ) and Standard Deviations (σ)

sections.heading: Item Response Theory (IRT)
sections.preview: Unlike Classical Test Theory (which focuses on total score), Item Response Theory focuses on the relationship between a person's latent ability (Theta or θ) and their probability of answering a specific item correctly. The EPPP is scored using IRT to ensure fairness across different versions of the exam. Parameter a (D

chapters.title: Psychopharmacokinetics & Neurotransmission
chapters.reviewNote: Corrected the synapse model and broken SVG marker, qualified SSRI mechanism, defined elimination half-life and the 4â€“5-half-life approximation with assumptions, removed generic washout advice, corrected fluoxetine label context, rejected AI/pharmacology equivalence, and added an explicit first-order calculation check.

sections.heading: The Synaptic Cleft and Neurotransmission
sections.preview: Neurotransmission is the process by which signaling molecules called neurotransmitters are released by a neuron (the presynaptic neuron) and bind to receptors on postsynaptic cells or presynaptic autoreceptors, producing excitatory, inhibitory, or modulatory effects. Reuptake, enzymatic degradation, diffusion, and glia
sections.diagramDescription: The Synapse: Neurotransmitter Release and Reuptake

sections.heading: Half-Life and Steady State
sections.preview: Half-life (t½) is the time for a measured drug concentration or amount to fall by half during the relevant elimination phase. For linear first-order kinetics, t1/2 relates to clearance and volume of distribution; dosing interval and clinical duration also depend on formulation, active metabolites, therapeutic window, p

diagrams.description: 🔬 A neuron showing dendrites (receive), soma (integrate), axon with myelin sheaths (conduct), and terminal buttons (release neurotransmitters). The green pulse shows the action potential traveling along the axon.
🧠 Classical conditioning: a neutral stimulus (bell) becomes a conditioned stimulus through repeated pairing with an unconditioned stimulus (food). The glowing box shows the active learning phase.

🔴 The HPA axis stress cascade: Hypothalamus → CRH → Pituitary → ACTH → Adrenal Cortex → Cortisol. The red dashed line shows the negative feedback loop. Chronic activation leads to hippocampal damage and mood disorders.

🧩 The four major brain lobes: Frontal (blue — executive function), Parietal (purple — sensory/spatial), Temporal (yellow — memory/language), Occipital (green — vision). Broca's and Wernicke's areas are highlighted with pulsing circles.

🧬 The four dopamine pathways: Mesocortical (VTA→PFC, cognition), Mesolimbic (VTA→NAc, reward/psychosis), Nigrostriatal (SN→Caudate, movement), Tuberoinfundibular (hypothalamus→pituitary, prolactin). Flowing dashes show signal direction.
📊 Erikson's 8 psychosocial stages from birth through old age, showing the core crisis and associated virtue at each stage. The green dot traces the lifespan journey.

📋 The 5-step differential diagnosis hierarchy: always rule out malingering, substances, and medical conditions BEFORE determining the primary mental disorder. The green scanner shows the systematic evaluation process.

📊 The Normal Distribution (Bell Curve). Shows the 68-95-99.7 rule. 1 Standard Deviation (±1 SD) contains ~68% of the population. 2 SDs contain ~95%. The green pulsing area highlights the 68% between -1 and +1 SD.

🐀 The Four Quadrants of Operant Conditioning. Remember: Positive means ADDING something, Negative means REMOVING something. Reinforcement INCREASES behavior, Punishment DECREASES behavior. The flashing green box highlights Negative Reinforcement (removing a bad thing to increase a behavior), which is highly tested and often confused with punishment.

👶 Piaget's Cognitive Stages: A staircase model. Sensorimotor (learn via senses/movement; achieve Object Permanence). Preoperational (symbols, language; but Egocentric and lacking conservation). Concrete Operational (logical thinking about physical objects; achieve Conservation). Formal Operational (abstract and hypothetical reasoning).

💊 Synaptic Transmission & Drug Mechanisms. SSRIs (like Prozac) block the reuptake pump, keeping more serotonin in the synapse. MAOIs block the monoamine oxidase (MAO) enzyme from destroying neurotransmitters. Both effectively increase neurotransmitter activity.

🧊 Freud's Structural Iceberg Model. The Ego represents conscious reality, the Superego spans all levels to represent morality, and the Id operates completely unconsciously on the pleasure principle. Water level indicates the threshold of consciousness.
🎯 The classic Target Analogy for Measurement. Reliability = Consistency (grouping of the hits). Validity = Accuracy (hitting the center bullseye). A test must be reliable before it can be valid.

🧠 The Atkinson-Shiffrin 3-Stage Memory Model. Sensory memory briefly holds exact environmental info. Paying ATTENTION moves it to Short-Term Memory (STM) where it loops via rehearsal. ENCODING moves it to permanent Long-Term Memory (LTM), and RETRIEVAL brings it back to STM for use.
🔺 Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs. Lower four levels are Deficiency Needs (D-Needs) — motivated by lack. The top level, Self-Actualization, is a Being Need (B-Need) — achieving one's full potential.

👁️ The Visual Pathway. Note the contralateral processing: The entirely LEFT Visual Field (Blue) hits the nasal retina of the left eye and temporal retina of the right eye, crossing at the Optic Chiasm to be processed in the RIGHT Occipital Lobe. Conversely, the entirely RIGHT Visual Field (Red) is processed in the LEFT Occipital Lobe. Damage before the chiasm affects one eye; damage after the chiasm affects one entire visual field.

⚡ Festinger's Cognitive Dissonance Theory. When our attitudes and behaviors clash, we experience an uncomfortable tension (dissonance). Because changing behavior (quitting smoking) is often difficult, people frequently resolve the tension by changing their attitude or rationalizing the behavior (e.g., adding a new belief like "smoking lowers my stress").

📊 Construct Validity Framework. Construct validity is the "umbrella" term asking if your measure actually captures the theoretical construct. Content validity asks if you sampled all parts of the subject. Criterion validity relies on external data (Concurrent = NOW, Predictive = FUTURE). Convergent/Discriminant validity ensures your construct correlates with similar traits but NOT with unrelated traits.

⚖️ Kohlberg's Stages of Moral Development. Focuses on the reasoning behind moral choices (like the Heinz Dilemma), not just the choice itself. Moves from avoiding punishment (Preconventional) to following society's rules (Conventional) to personal, universal ethics (Postconventional). Carol Gilligan famously criticized Kohlberg for neglecting the "ethics of care" more prevalent in women.

🔺 Cognitive-Behavioral Therapy holds that psychological distress is maintained by the reciprocal interactions between core beliefs, automatic thoughts, emotional states, and behavioral responses. Intervening at any corner of the triangle impacts the others.

🪜 Systematic Desensitization relies on 'reciprocal inhibition' (anxiety and relaxation are physiologically mutually exclusive). The therapist builds a graduated fear hierarchy, trains the client in deep relaxation, and successfully pairs the relaxation response with each escalating exposure step.

☯️ Dialectical Behavior Therapy (DBT) relies heavily on four core skills modules that balance the central dialectic of Acceptance (Mindfulness & Distress Tolerance) and Change (Emotion Regulation & Interpersonal Effectiveness).

👥 Asch Conformity Experiment. Demonstrated the power of normative social influence. Participants conformed to the clearly incorrect majority answer on about 32% of trials. Conformity dropped sharply if even ONE confederate gave the correct answer (breaking unanimity) or if the participant could write their answer privately.

🛑 Bystander Intervention Tree. A prosocial act requires completing ALL 5 steps. "Pluralistic Ignorance" breaks Step 2 (if no one acts, you assume it's not an emergency). "Diffusion of Responsibility" breaks Step 3 (assuming someone else called 911).

🧬 Autonomic Nervous System. Remember: "Sympathy" for a stressful situation (Sympathetic = Fight/Flight). A "Parachute" calms you down (Parasympathetic = Rest/Digest). Everything the sympathetic nervous system turns on during an emergency (heart rate, breathing), the parasympathetic reverses when safe.

flashcards.front: What are the two divisions of the nervous system?
flashcards.back: Central Nervous System (CNS: brain + spinal cord) and Peripheral Nervous System (PNS: somatic + autonomic)
Speech production (left frontal lobe). Damage causes Broca's aphasia — nonfluent, effortful speech with intact comprehension.
Language comprehension (left temporal lobe). Damage causes fluent but meaningless speech with poor comprehension.

flashcards.front: What is the function of the hippocampus?
flashcards.back: Memory consolidation — transferring short-term to long-term memory. Damage causes anterograde amnesia (e.g., Patient H.M.).

flashcards.front: What neurotransmitter is most associated with Parkinson's disease?
flashcards.back: Dopamine — degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra causes motor symptoms.

flashcards.front: What is the mechanism of SSRIs?
flashcards.back: Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors block the reuptake of serotonin, increasing its availability in the synaptic cleft.
Strengthening of synaptic connections through repeated stimulation — the neural basis of learning and memory.
A psychological model stating that disorders result from the interaction of genetic vulnerability (diathesis) and environmental stressors.

flashcards.front: What is the primary inhibitory neurotransmitter?
flashcards.back: GABA (gamma-aminobutyric acid). Low levels associated with anxiety. Benzodiazepines enhance GABA activity.
Processes emotions, especially fear and aggression. Central to fear conditioning and emotional memory.
Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Adrenal axis: hypothalamus releases CRH → pituitary releases ACTH → adrenal cortex releases cortisol. Chronic activation linked to depression and immune suppression.

Broca's (left frontal): speech production. Damage → non-fluent aphasia (effortful, telegraphic speech, comprehension intact). Wernicke's (left temporal): speech comprehension. Damage → fluent but meaningless speech.

flashcards.front: What neurotransmitter is depleted in Parkinson's disease?
flashcards.back: Dopamine — specifically from degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra. Produces resting tremor, bradykinesia, rigidity, and postural instability.
Typical (1st gen): block D2 dopamine receptors. Risk of EPS and tardive dyskinesia. Atypical (2nd gen): block both D2 and 5-HT2A serotonin receptors. Lower EPS risk, but metabolic side effects.
Formed by endothelial cell tight junctions supported by astrocyte foot processes. Selectively allows lipid-soluble substances to pass while blocking large water-soluble molecules.
Thiamine (Vitamin B1) deficiency, usually from chronic alcoholism. Wernicke's: confusion, ataxia, ophthalmoplegia. Korsakoff's: severe anterograde amnesia, confabulation.
Failure to attend to stimuli on one side (usually left) despite intact vision. Caused by damage to the right parietal lobe. The right hemisphere processes spatial attention for both fields.

Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors block the serotonin transporter (SERT), preventing reuptake into the presynaptic neuron. Increases serotonin availability in the synaptic cleft. Examples: fluoxetine (Prozac), sertraline (Zoloft), escitalopram (Lexapro).

flashcards.front: What is the difference between MAOIs and SSRIs?
flashcards.back: MAOIs inhibit monoamine oxidase enzyme, preventing breakdown of serotonin, norepinephrine, AND dopamine — broad effect. SSRIs selectively block serotonin reuptake only. MAOIs: dietary restrictions (tyramine → hypertensive crisis). SSRIs: fewer side effects, first-line treatment.

Mood stabilizer for bipolar disorder. Mechanism not fully understood — affects multiple neurotransmitter systems, second messengers, and neuroprotective pathways. Narrow therapeutic window (0.6–1.2 mEq/L). Requires regular blood level monitoring. Toxicity risk: tremor, confusion, seizures.

Benzos (diazepam, lorazepam): fast-acting, GABA-A agonists. Risk: dependence, sedation, withdrawal seizures. Buspirone: 5-HT1A partial agonist, slower onset (2-4 weeks), NO dependence/sedation risk, no abuse potential. Buspirone preferred for chronic GAD.

Methylphenidate- and amphetamine-based medications increase catecholamine signaling and can reduce core ADHD symptoms for many patients. Response and adverse effects vary; prescribing requires assessment and monitoring. Treatment may also include behavioral, family, school, organizational, or other supports.

Broca's (expressive/non-fluent): impaired speech production, intact comprehension. Telegraphic speech. Left inferior frontal gyrus. Wernicke's (receptive/fluent): fluent but meaningless speech, impaired comprehension. Left superior temporal gyrus.

flashcards.front: What is the reticular activating system (RAS)?
flashcards.back: Network in the brainstem (medulla through midbrain) that regulates arousal, wakefulness, and attention. Damage → coma or persistent vegetative state. Filters sensory input to prevent cortical overload. Key role in sleep-wake cycle.

Sympathetic: fight-or-flight. Dilates pupils, increases HR, inhibits digestion, releases epinephrine. Thoracolumbar origin. Parasympathetic: rest-and-digest. Constricts pupils, decreases HR, promotes digestion. Craniosacral origin. Vagus nerve (CN X) = major parasympathetic nerve.

flashcards.front: Neurotransmitter imbalance theories of mental illness
flashcards.back: Depression: low serotonin/norepinephrine. Schizophrenia: excess dopamine (mesolimbic) and low dopamine (mesocortical). Anxiety: low GABA. Alzheimer's: low acetylcholine. OCD: serotonin dysfunction. Parkinson's: low dopamine (nigrostriatal). Simplified models — reality is more complex.

Neurons that fire both when performing an action AND when observing another perform it. Discovered in macaque premotor cortex (Rizzolatti). Theorized to be involved in empathy, imitation, and social cognition. Their role in autism is debated.

The brain's 'relay station' — routes nearly all sensory information to appropriate cortical areas. Exception: olfaction (smell) goes directly to cortex. Also involved in consciousness, sleep, and alertness. Damage → widespread sensory deficits.

Traditional: coordinates movement, balance, posture, motor learning. Newer research: also involved in timing, language processing, cognitive functions, and emotional regulation. Contains ~80% of all brain neurons. Damage → ataxia, intention tremor, dysarthria.

Processes emotions (especially fear), emotional memory, and threat detection. Key role in fear conditioning. Hyperactivity linked to anxiety disorders and PTSD. Damage (Klüver-Bucy syndrome): placidity, hyperorality, visual agnosia, hypersexuality.

Largest white matter tract connecting left and right hemispheres. Severed in split-brain surgery for epilepsy. Sperry/Gazzaniga showed: left hemisphere = language/speech; right = spatial/holistic. Disconnected hemispheres can have independent awareness.

flashcards.front: Antipsychotic side effects: EPS vs. metabolic
flashcards.back: Typical (1st gen) EPS: dystonia, akathisia, parkinsonism, tardive dyskinesia (involuntary movements — potentially irreversible). Atypical (2nd gen) metabolic: weight gain, diabetes, dyslipidemia. Clozapine: most effective for treatment-resistant schizophrenia but risk of agranulocytosis.

SSRIs (fluoxetine, sertraline) → first-line. SNRIs (venlafaxine, duloxetine) → depression + pain. TCAs (amitriptyline, imipramine) → older, cardiotoxic in OD. MAOIs (phenelzine, tranylcypromine) → tyramine restrictions. Atypicals (bupropion, mirtazapine, trazodone).

Benzodiazepines (alprazolam, clonazepam): fast-acting, dependence risk. Buspirone: 5-HT1A partial agonist, no dependence, slow onset. SSRIs/SNRIs: first-line for chronic anxiety. Hydroxyzine: antihistamine, PRN. Beta-blockers (propranolol): performance anxiety only.

Lithium: classic, narrow therapeutic index, requires blood monitoring. Valproate (Depakote): also for seizures, hepatotoxic. Carbamazepine (Tegretol): also for seizures, blood dyscrasias. Lamotrigine (Lamictal): prevents depressive episodes, risk of SJS rash. Atypical antipsychotics: increasingly used.

Stimulants: methylphenidate (Ritalin/Concerta), amphetamines (Adderall/Vyvanse) — first-line, fast onset, abuse potential. Non-stimulants: atomoxetine (Strattera, NRI), guanfacine (Intuniv, alpha-2 agonist), clonidine. Non-stimulants preferred when abuse risk or comorbid tics.

Clozapine is used for treatment-resistant schizophrenia and can reduce recurrent suicidal behavior in schizophrenia or schizoaffective disorder. Serious risks include severe neutropenia, myocarditis, seizures, and metabolic effects. The U.S. REMS was removed in 2025, but FDA labeling still recommends ANC monitoring at specified frequencies.
