memoryAids.content: **Unique ethical challenges in rural practice:** 🏘️ **Multiple relationships are often UNAVOIDABLE:** → Your client is also your child's teacher → Your client's spouse is your mechanic → You're the ONLY psychologist in 100 miles 📋 **APA guidance:** → Multiple relationships are NOT automatically unethical → Unethical only if they IMPAIR objectivity or risk EXPLOITATION → Document your reasoning for entering the relationship 🛡️ **Best practices:** → Discuss potential complications with the client IN ADVANCE → Consult with colleagues (even by phone/telehealth) → Maintain clear professional boundaries → Consider the client's welfare FIRST → Be mindful of confidentiality in small towns ("I saw you at the therapist's office" = outing) **Key EPPP scenario:** → Rural psychologist asked to see neighbor's child → Best answer: NOT automatic refusal — EVALUATE the situation → Can proceed if no impairment of objectivity → But: NEVER sexual relationships, even in rural areas!

memoryAids.title: Malpractice Elements
memoryAids.content: **"The 4 D's" — ALL 4 required to prove malpractice:** 📋 **D**uty = Professional relationship existed → You were their psychologist (duty of care) → No duty to random strangers (usually) → Exception: Tarasoff duty to identifiable victims ❌ **D**ereliction = You breached the standard of care → Did something a reasonable psychologist would NOT do → OR failed to do something a reasonable psychologist WOULD do → Compared to peers, not perfection 💥 **D**amage = The client was actually HARMED → Emotional, physical, or financial injury → No harm = no malpractice (even if you made a mistake!) 🔗 **D**irect causation = YOUR breach CAUSED the harm → Must prove a direct link between dereliction and damage → Client's pre-existing condition may complicate this **Most common malpractice claims:** → Sexual relationships with clients (#1) → Breach of confidentiality → Failure to warn/protect (Tarasoff) → Improper diagnosis or treatment → Abandonment of client

memoryAids.title: Psychotropic Medication Classes
memoryAids.content: **"AMAS" — 4 major classes of psychotropic meds:** 💊 **A**ntidepressants = Treat depression, anxiety, OCD → SSRIs (fluoxetine/Prozac, sertraline/Zoloft) — 1st line! → SNRIs (venlafaxine/Effexor, duloxetine/Cymbalta) → TCAs (amitriptyline, imipramine) — older, more side effects → MAOIs (phenelzine/Nardil) — tyramine diet restriction! → Onset: 2-6 weeks to take effect 🧠 **M**ood Stabilizers = Bipolar disorder → Lithium = gold standard (needs blood monitoring!) → Valproic acid (Depakote), carbamazepine, lamotrigine → Lithium toxicity: tremor, confusion, seizures 😰 **A**nxiolytics = Anxiety disorders → Benzodiazepines (diazepam/Valium, alprazolam/Xanax) → Fast-acting BUT addictive! (schedule IV) → Buspirone (BuSpar) = non-addictive alternative (slower onset) 🧩 **A**ntipsychotics = Psychotic disorders → Typical/1st gen (haloperidol): block D2 dopamine → EPS risk → Atypical/2nd gen (risperidone, clozapine): fewer EPS → Clozapine for treatment-resistant schizophrenia (agranulocytosis risk!)

memoryAids.title: Antidepressant Mechanisms
memoryAids.content: **How each class works — using a bathtub analogy:** 🛁 Think of serotonin/norepinephrine as water in a bathtub (synapse): → Too little water = depression → Goal: keep more water in the tub! 🔌 **SSRIs** = Plug the drain (block reuptake of serotonin) → Selective: ONLY serotonin → Fewest side effects → first-line treatment → Side effects: sexual dysfunction, GI issues, weight gain 🔌🔌 **SNRIs** = Plug TWO drains (serotonin + norepinephrine) → Useful for pain conditions too → "Dual action" antidepressants 🔌🔌🔌 **TCAs** = Plug MULTIPLE drains (less selective) → Block serotonin, NE, AND other receptors → More side effects: cardiac, anticholinergic → Dangerous in overdose (cardiac arrest!) 🚫 **MAOIs** = Turn off the enzyme that BREAKS DOWN water → MAO enzyme normally degrades serotonin, NE, dopamine → MAOIs prevent breakdown → more neurotransmitter available → Tyramine restriction: wine, cheese, aged meats → hypertensive crisis! **Key:** All take 2-6 weeks for full effect (neuroplastic changes)

memoryAids.title: Brain Lobes & Functions
memoryAids.content: **"FTPO" — 4 lobes, front to back:** 🎭 **F**rontal Lobe = The CEO → Executive function, planning, decision-making → Motor cortex (precentral gyrus) = movement → Broca's area (LEFT) = speech PRODUCTION → Personality, impulse control, working memory → Damage: Phineas Gage — personality change, disinhibition 👂 **T**emporal Lobe = The Librarian → Auditory processing → Wernicke's area (LEFT) = language COMPREHENSION → Hippocampus = memory formation → Amygdala = emotional processing (fear!) 🖐️ **P**arietal Lobe = The Navigator → Somatosensory cortex (postcentral gyrus) = touch, pain, temp → Spatial processing, math → Neglect syndrome: right parietal damage → ignore left side 👁️ **O**ccipital Lobe = The Camera → Visual processing (primary visual cortex) → Damage: cortical blindness, visual agnosia **Lateralization:** → LEFT = language, logic, detail (in most right-handers) → RIGHT = spatial, emotional, holistic, music

memoryAids.title: Aphasia Types
memoryAids.content: **"Broca = Broken speech, Wernicke = Wrong words"** 🗣️ **Broca's Aphasia** (Expressive / Non-fluent): → KNOWS what they want to say but CAN'T produce it → Speech is effortful, halting, telegraphic → "Want... go... store" (grammar stripped out) → Comprehension is INTACT → Location: LEFT frontal lobe (Broca's area) → Often accompanied by right-side paralysis 👂 **Wernicke's Aphasia** (Receptive / Fluent): → Speech FLOWS easily but makes NO sense → "Word salad" — fluent but meaningless → "The shing dog was plocking on the blurner" → Comprehension is IMPAIRED → Location: LEFT temporal lobe (Wernicke's area) → Patient often UNAWARE of their deficit (anosognosia) 🔗 **Conduction Aphasia:** → Can speak AND comprehend but can't REPEAT → Damage to arcuate fasciculus (connects Broca's & Wernicke's) 🌐 **Global Aphasia:** → BOTH production AND comprehension impaired → Most severe form — large left hemisphere damage

memoryAids.title: Motivation Theories — I/O
memoryAids.content: **5 essential workplace motivation theories:** 📊 **Maslow's Hierarchy** = 5 levels (bottom to top): → Physiological → Safety → Belongingness → Esteem → Self-actualization → Must satisfy LOWER needs before higher → Criticism: Too rigid, not well-supported empirically 🔄 **Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory:** → **Hygiene factors** (prevent dissatisfaction): pay, conditions, supervision → **Motivators** (create satisfaction): achievement, recognition, growth → Key: Removing dissatisfaction ≠ creating satisfaction! ⚖️ **Equity Theory** (Adams): → Compare your input/output ratio to others' → Perceived inequity → motivation to restore balance → Underpayment → reduce effort; Overpayment → increase effort 🎯 **Expectancy Theory** (Vroom) — VIE: → **V**alence × **I**nstrumentality × **E**xpectancy = Motivation → Do I value it? Will performance lead to reward? Can I do it? 🏆 **Goal-Setting Theory** (Locke): → SMART goals: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound → Difficult + specific goals → BEST performance

memoryAids.title: Leadership Theories
memoryAids.content: **Evolution of leadership theory — from traits to situations:** 👤 **Trait Theory** = Leaders are BORN (Great Man theory) → Intelligence, dominance, self-confidence → Problem: no universal trait predicts leadership 📋 **Behavioral Theories:** → Ohio State: Initiating Structure vs Consideration → Michigan: Task-oriented vs Employee-oriented → Best leaders score HIGH on both dimensions 🔄 **Contingency/Situational:** → **Fiedler's LPC:** Task-oriented leaders best in HIGH/LOW control → Relationship-oriented best in MODERATE control → Leader style is FIXED — change the situation! → **Hersey-Blanchard:** Match style to follower maturity → Telling → Selling → Participating → Delegating 🌟 **Transformational** (Burns, Bass): → Inspire through vision, charisma, intellectual stimulation → 4 I's: Idealized influence, Inspirational motivation, Intellectual stimulation, Individualized consideration → vs **Transactional** = Reward/punish based on performance 📊 **Leader-Member Exchange (LMX):** → In-group (trusted) vs Out-group (formal only)

memoryAids.title: Job Analysis Methods
memoryAids.content: **"The foundation of all HR functions"** 📋 **What is job analysis?** → Systematic study of tasks, duties, and responsibilities → Foundation for: hiring, training, evaluation, compensation 🔧 **Two orientations:** → **Task-oriented** = WHAT is done (tasks, duties) → **Worker-oriented** = WHO does it (KSAOs) → Knowledge, Skills, Abilities, Other characteristics 📊 **Key methods:** → **PAQ** (Position Analysis Questionnaire) — McCormick → 194 elements, worker-oriented, standardized → Most widely researched instrument → **FJA** (Functional Job Analysis) — Fine → Tasks rated on Data, People, Things → **CIT** (Critical Incident Technique) — Flanagan → Collect examples of effective/ineffective behavior → **O*NET** = Modern replacement for DOT → Online database of occupational info **Job evaluation** = Determine relative WORTH of jobs (for pay) → Different from job analysis (which describes the job)

memoryAids.title: Language Development Milestones
memoryAids.content: **"BCTH-TE" — Language milestones by age:** 👶 **0-2 months: Cooing** → Vowel-like sounds: "oooh" "aaah" 🗣️ **4-6 months: Babbling** → Consonant-vowel combos: "ba-ba" "da-da" → Deaf babies babble too (but it decreases) → Canonical babbling: repeated syllables 👆 **8-12 months: First words** → Holophrases = single word = whole sentence → "Up!" means "Pick me up please!" → Receptive > expressive (understand more than they say) 📝 **18-24 months: Two-word stage** → Telegraphic speech: "Daddy go" "More milk" → Vocabulary explosion (~50 words → 200+ rapidly) → Overextension: all animals = "doggy" → Underextension: only MY dog = "doggy" 🗨️ **3-5 years: Complex sentences** → Overgeneralization: "I goed" "I runned" → This shows RULE learning (not just imitation!) **Theories:** Skinner (learning) vs Chomsky (LAD/nativist) vs Interactionist

memoryAids.title: Vygotsky's Key Concepts
memoryAids.content: **Lev Vygotsky — Social construction of knowledge:** 🎯 **Zone of Proximal Development (ZPD):** → Gap between what child can do ALONE vs with HELP → "Just-right challenge" zone → Learning happens IN the ZPD → Too easy = boredom; Too hard = frustration 🏗️ **Scaffolding** (Wood, Bruner, Ross — not Vygotsky himself!): → Temporary support that is gradually removed → Teacher adjusts help as child gains competence → Like training wheels on a bike 💬 **Private Speech:** → Children talk to themselves to guide behavior → "Now I put the red one here..." → NOT egocentric (Piaget) — it's FUNCTIONAL → Becomes inner speech (internalized) by ~7 🌍 **Social learning precedes individual:** → "Every function appears TWICE: first SOCIAL, then INDIVIDUAL" → Interpsychological → Intrapsychological **Vygotsky vs Piaget:** → V: Social → Individual (culture drives development) → P: Individual → Social (child discovers independently) → V: Language drives thought; P: Thought drives language

memoryAids.title: Personality Assessment Instruments
memoryAids.content: **Big 5 personality assessments for the EPPP:** 📋 **MMPI-3** (Restructured, 2020): → 335 items, True/False → Validity scales: F, L, K (detect faking!) → Clinical scales + RC scales + PSY-5 → Objective, empirically keyed (not theory-based) → Ages 18+ (MMPI-A for adolescents) 🖼️ **Rorschach** (Inkblot): → 10 symmetrical inkblots → Exner's Comprehensive System → now R-PAS → Projective test — measures thought process → Controversial reliability/validity 🖼️ **TAT** (Thematic Apperception Test — Murray): → Ambiguous picture scenes → tell a story → Reveals needs, motives, conflicts (Murray's need theory) → Projective test 📊 **NEO-PI-R** (Costa & McCrae): → Measures Big Five: OCEAN → Openness, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Agreeableness, Neuroticism → 240 items, well-validated **Objective vs Projective:** → Objective: structured, standardized, reliable (MMPI) → Projective: ambiguous, reveals unconscious (Rorschach, TAT)

memoryAids.title: Neuropsychological Assessment
memoryAids.content: **Key neuropsych batteries & tests for EPPP:** 🧠 **Halstead-Reitan Battery:** → Comprehensive, fixed battery (same tests for everyone) → Impairment Index: 0-1 (higher = more impairment) → Includes Category Test, TPT, Finger Tapping → Good for lateralization (left vs right damage) 🧠 **Luria-Nebraska Battery:** → Briefer than Halstead-Reitan → Based on Luria's theory of brain function → 11 scales including motor, rhythm, visual → Better for qualitative analysis 📊 **Individual tests:** → **Wisconsin Card Sorting Test (WCST):** → Measures executive function / cognitive flexibility → Sensitive to FRONTAL LOBE damage → Perseveration = key error pattern → **Trail Making Test (A & B):** → Part A: connect numbers (processing speed) → Part B: alternate numbers & letters (set shifting) → Part B sensitive to frontal lobe function → **Bender-Gestalt** (Bender Visual Motor Gestalt Test): → Copy geometric designs → Screens for brain damage + development → Also used with children (developmental level)

memoryAids.title: Group Therapy — Yalom's Therapeutic Factors
memoryAids.content: **Irvin Yalom's 11 Therapeutic Factors in group therapy:** 🌟 **"HUGE CAIRS" + 3 more:** → **H**ope installation = Seeing others improve → **U**niversality = "I'm not the only one!" → **G**roup cohesiveness = Bonding, belonging → **E**xistential factors = Accepting life's limitations → **C**atharsis = Emotional release/expression → **A**ltruism = Helping others helps YOU → **I**nterpersonal learning = Feedback from group members → **R**ecapitulation of family = Group recreates family dynamics → **S**ocializing techniques = Learn social skills → **I**mitative behavior = Model after others → **I**mparting information = Psychoeducation **Stages of group (Tuckman):** → Forming → Storming → Norming → Performing → Adjourning **Key:** Universality and hope are especially important EARLY Cohesion is the group equivalent of therapeutic alliance

memoryAids.title: Solution-Focused Brief Therapy
memoryAids.content: **Steve de Shazer & Insoo Kim Berg — Key techniques:** ✨ **Miracle Question:** → "If a miracle happened tonight and your problem was solved..." → "...what would be DIFFERENT tomorrow?" → Shifts focus from problem to SOLUTION → Helps client envision desired future 📊 **Scaling Questions:** → "On a scale of 1-10, where are you now?" → "What would it take to move from a 5 to a 6?" → Makes progress concrete and measurable 🔍 **Exception Finding:** → "When is the problem NOT happening?" → Find times when it's already better → do MORE of that → Exceptions = existing strengths/solutions 👏 **Compliments & Coping Questions:** → "How have you managed to cope so well?" → Highlight existing strengths and resilience **Key principles:** → Clients are the EXPERTS on their own lives → Focus on what's WORKING, not what's broken → Small changes lead to big changes → Brief: typically 3-5 sessions → No formal diagnosis needed; atheoretical about cause

memoryAids.title: DBT — Dialectical Behavior Therapy
memoryAids.content: **Marsha Linehan — Originally for Borderline Personality Disorder:** 💡 **Core dialectic:** Acceptance AND Change (both/and, not either/or) 🎯 **4 Skills modules — "DIME":** 🧘 **D**istress Tolerance = Survive crisis without making it worse → TIPP: Temperature, Intense exercise, Paced breathing, Paired muscle relaxation → Radical acceptance: "It is what it is" 🤝 **I**nterpersonal Effectiveness = Get what you need in relationships → DEAR MAN: Describe, Express, Assert, Reinforce, Mindful, Appear confident, Negotiate → Balance priorities, self-respect, relationships 🧠 **M**indfulness = Core skill (foundation for all others) → Observe, Describe, Participate → Non-judgmentally, One-mindfully, Effectively → "Wise mind" = intersection of emotion mind + reasonable mind 😊 **E**motion Regulation = Reduce vulnerability to negative emotions → ABC PLEASE: Accumulate positives, Build mastery, Cope ahead → PLEASE: Physical health (treat PhysicaL illness, balanced Eating, avoid mood-Altering substances, balanced Sleep, Exercise) **Treatment structure:** Individual therapy + Skills group + Phone coaching + Consultation team

memoryAids.title: Type I vs Type II Errors
memoryAids.content: **"The Boy Who Cried Wolf" analogy:** 🐺 **Type I Error (α / Alpha) = FALSE ALARM** → Reject H₀ when it's actually TRUE → "Crying wolf" — saying there's an effect when there ISN'T → Conclusion: "It works!" Reality: "No, it doesn't." → Controlled by setting alpha level (usually .05) → p < .05 means: "Less than 5% chance this is a Type I error" 😴 **Type II Error (β / Beta) = MISS** → Fail to reject H₀ when it's actually FALSE → "Missing the wolf" — saying there's NO effect when there IS → Conclusion: "It doesn't work." Reality: "Yes, it does." → Related to statistical POWER (1 − β) ⚡ **Power** = Probability of correctly detecting a real effect → Increase power by: larger N, larger effect, higher α, less variability → Typical goal: power ≥ .80 (80%) **Tradeoff:** Reducing Type I risk → increases Type II risk (and vice versa) **Mnemonic:** Type I = False Positive (I = "I see something that's not there") Type II = False Negative (II = "It's there but I missed it")

memoryAids.title: Effect Size Interpretation
memoryAids.content: **"Small, Medium, Large" — Cohen's benchmarks:** 📊 **Cohen's d** (mean difference in SD units): → Small = 0.2 (barely noticeable) → Medium = 0.5 (noticeable in everyday life) → Large = 0.8 (very obvious difference) → d = (M₁ - M₂) / SD_pooled 📊 **Pearson's r** (correlation / effect size): → Small = .10 (r² = 1% variance explained) → Medium = .30 (r² = 9% variance explained) → Large = .50 (r² = 25% variance explained) 📊 **Eta-squared (η²)** (for ANOVA): → Small = .01 → Medium = .06 → Large = .14 → Proportion of DV variance explained by IV **Why effect size matters:** → Statistical significance ≠ practical significance! → Large N can make TINY effects "significant" → APA requires reporting effect size (since 2001) → Meta-analyses use effect sizes to combine studies **r² = coefficient of determination** = % of variance explained → r = .50 → r² = .25 → accounts for 25% of variance

memoryAids.title: Informed Consent Elements
memoryAids.content: **"PRINCDT" — 7 essential elements of informed consent:** 📋 **P**urpose of treatment/evaluation → What will we be doing and why? ⏰ **R**isks and benefits → Potential harms AND potential gains → Must disclose REASONABLY FORESEEABLE risks 🔒 **I**nformation about confidentiality → What's private and what's NOT (limits!) → Mandated reporting, Tarasoff, subpoenas 💰 **N**ature of fees and billing → Session cost, insurance, cancellation policy → Collection procedures for unpaid balances 🚪 **C**hoice — voluntary participation → Right to refuse or withdraw at ANY time → Without penalty or negative consequences 📞 **D**uration and alternatives → Expected length of treatment → Alternative treatment options available ❓ **T**herapist qualifications → Training, licensure, areas of competence → Supervision status if trainee **Special populations:** → Minors: parent/guardian consents; child gives ASSENT → Court-ordered: still need IC but limits on confidentiality differ → Cognitive impairment: consent from legal guardian

memoryAids.title: Supervision Models
memoryAids.content: **3 major supervision models for EPPP:** 📋 **Developmental Models:** → **IDM** (Integrated Developmental Model — Stoltenberg): → Level 1: Anxious, rigid, imitative (need structure) → Level 2: Fluctuating confidence, autonomous struggles → Level 3: Stable identity, flexible, "master clinician" → Level 3i: Integrated across domains → Supervisor adjusts style to match supervisee's level 🔄 **Discrimination Model** (Bernard): → 3 Roles: Teacher, Counselor, Consultant → 3 Focus areas: Process, Conceptualization, Personalization → Supervisor shifts roles based on what's needed → Most flexible/atheoretical model 🧠 **Psychotherapy-Based Models:** → Match supervision to therapy orientation → Psychodynamic: parallel process, transference in supervision → CBT: structured, skill-focused, homework → Person-centered: empathy, genuineness in supervision **Key ethics of supervision:** → Supervisor has VICARIOUS LIABILITY for supervisee's clients → Supervisor must be competent in supervisee's treatment methods → Dual relationships with supervisees: power differential!

memoryAids.title: Personality Disorder Clusters
memoryAids.content: **"Mad, Bad, Sad" — the 3 clusters:** 🌀 **Cluster A — ODD / Eccentric ("Mad"):** → **Paranoid** = Suspicious, distrustful, reads malice everywhere → **Schizoid** = Loner, flat affect, doesn't WANT relationships → **Schizotypal** = Odd beliefs, magical thinking, eccentric → Think: related to schizophrenia spectrum 🔥 **Cluster B — DRAMATIC / Erratic ("Bad"):** → **Antisocial** = Disregard for rights of others, deceit, no remorse → Must be 18+; Conduct Disorder before age 15 → **Borderline** = Unstable relationships, identity, emotions; fear of abandonment → Splitting, self-harm, chronic emptiness → **Histrionic** = Attention-seeking, excessive emotion, theatrical → **Narcissistic** = Grandiosity, need for admiration, lack of empathy 😰 **Cluster C — ANXIOUS / Fearful ("Sad"):** → **Avoidant** = WANTS relationships but fears rejection (shy) → **Dependent** = Clingy, submissive, can't make decisions alone → **Obsessive-Compulsive PD** = Rigid, perfectionist, controlling → NOT same as OCD! (OCPD is ego-syntonic) **Most commonly tested:** Borderline, Antisocial, Narcissistic

memoryAids.title: Schizophrenia Spectrum
memoryAids.content: **Positive vs Negative symptoms + related disorders:** ➕ **Positive symptoms** (added to normal experience): → Hallucinations (auditory most common — hearing voices) → Delusions (persecutory most common) → Disorganized speech (loose associations, tangentiality) → Disorganized/catatonic behavior → Respond BETTER to medication (antipsychotics) ➖ **Negative symptoms** (subtracted from normal): → Flat affect (reduced emotional expression) → Alogia (poverty of speech) → Avolition (lack of motivation/initiative) → Anhedonia (can't feel pleasure) → Asociality (social withdrawal) → HARDER to treat, predict WORSE prognosis 📊 **DSM-5 Spectrum:** → Schizophrenia: 6+ months, 1+ month active symptoms → Schizophreniform: 1-6 months (same symptoms) → Brief Psychotic Disorder: < 1 month → Schizoaffective: schizophrenia + mood episode → Delusional Disorder: delusions ONLY (no other symptoms) **Dopamine hypothesis:** Excess dopamine in mesolimbic pathway → positive symptoms Too LITTLE dopamine in mesocortical pathway → negative symptoms

memoryAids.title: Sleep Stages
memoryAids.content: **NREM → REM cycle (~90 minutes):** 😴 **Stage 1 (N1):** Light sleep (5%) → Theta waves → Hypnagogic hallucinations (vivid images) → Myoclonic jerks ("falling" sensation) → Easy to wake up 💤 **Stage 2 (N2):** True sleep onset (45-50%) → Sleep spindles + K-complexes → Spindles = memory consolidation! → Most of the night spent here 🌊 **Stage 3 (N3):** Deep/Slow-Wave Sleep (15-20%) → Delta waves (slow, high amplitude) → MOST restorative physically → Growth hormone released → Hardest to wake up → Parasomnias occur here: sleepwalking, night terrors, bedwetting 🎬 **REM Sleep** (20-25%): → Rapid eye movements, vivid DREAMS → Muscle atonia (paralyzed — prevents acting out dreams) → EEG looks like AWAKE brain (paradoxical sleep) → Memory consolidation (procedural + emotional) → REM increases across the night → REM rebound: REM deprivation → MORE REM next night **Key:** Infants ~50% REM; elderly less deep sleep (N3)

memoryAids.title: Split-Brain Research
memoryAids.content: **Sperry & Gazzaniga — What happens when you cut the corpus callosum?** ✂️ **Corpus callosum** = Bridge connecting left & right hemispheres → Cut to treat severe epilepsy (split-brain surgery) → Each hemisphere works INDEPENDENTLY after surgery 👁️ **Visual field trick:** → LEFT visual field → RIGHT hemisphere → RIGHT visual field → LEFT hemisphere → (Optic chiasm crosses the info) 🧪 **Classic experiment:** → Flash "KEY" to LEFT visual field (goes to RIGHT hemisphere) → Patient says: "I didn't see anything" (LEFT hemisphere speaks) → BUT: Left hand picks up a key! (RIGHT hemisphere controls left hand) 🧠 **Lateralization summary:** → **LEFT hemisphere:** Language, logic, math, analysis → Broca's & Wernicke's areas → "The interpreter" (makes up explanations) → **RIGHT hemisphere:** Spatial, emotion, music, face recognition → Holistic processing, creativity → Better at detecting deception **Key:** Split-brain patients can have conflicts between hemispheres → "Alien hand syndrome" — one hand acts against the other!

memoryAids.title: Signal Detection Theory
memoryAids.content: **SDT — Beyond simple thresholds:** 📊 **4 possible outcomes when detecting a signal:** | | Signal Present | Signal Absent | |---|---|---| | Say "Yes" | ✅ HIT | ❌ FALSE ALARM | | Say "No" | ❌ MISS | ✅ CORRECT REJECTION | 🎯 **Two key factors:** → **Sensitivity (d')** = Ability to distinguish signal from noise → Higher d' = better at detecting signals → Determined by signal strength and observer ability → **Response bias (β)** = Tendency to say "yes" or "no" → Liberal bias: say "yes" often (more hits BUT more false alarms) → Conservative bias: say "no" often (fewer false alarms BUT more misses) 📋 **Real-world examples:** → Radiologist reading X-rays (tumor or not?) → Eyewitness lineup (is the perpetrator there?) → Airport security (threat or harmless?) **Key:** Performance depends on BOTH sensitivity AND decision criteria → Not just about ability — motivation and consequences matter!

memoryAids.title: Schedules of Reinforcement
memoryAids.content: **"FVFV" — 4 partial reinforcement schedules:** ⏰ **Fixed Ratio (FR)** = Reinforce after set NUMBER of responses → Example: Pay per 10 widgets assembled → Pattern: Pause after reinforcement, then rapid responding → "Post-reinforcement pause" 🎰 **Variable Ratio (VR)** = Reinforce after AVERAGE number of responses → Example: Slot machines, fishing, sales calls → Pattern: HIGHEST + STEADIEST response rate! → MOST resistant to extinction → "You never know when the next one will pay off" ⏱️ **Fixed Interval (FI)** = Reinforce first response after set TIME → Example: Checking mail, weekly paycheck → Pattern: "Scallop" — slow start, speeds up near interval end → Studying intensifies before exam 🎲 **Variable Interval (VI)** = Reinforce first response after AVERAGE time → Example: Checking email, pop quizzes → Pattern: Slow, STEADY responding → Very resistant to extinction **Key:** Variable schedules = MORE resistant to extinction Ratio schedules = HIGHER response rates **Partial Reinforcement Extinction Effect (PREE):** Partial > Continuous for resistance

memoryAids.title: Bandura's Observational Learning
memoryAids.content: **"ARRM" — 4 steps of modeling (learning by watching):** 👀 **A**ttention = Must WATCH the model → Attractive, powerful, similar models get more attention → Celebrities, parents, peers = effective models 🧠 **R**etention = Must REMEMBER what you saw → Store mental representation of behavior → Rehearsal (mental or physical) helps 🏃 **R**eproduction = Must be ABLE to do it → Physical capability to perform the behavior → Practice needed to refine skill 🎯 **M**otivation = Must WANT to do it → Vicarious reinforcement: model was rewarded → I'll try it → Vicarious punishment: model was punished → I won't → Self-efficacy: "Do I believe I CAN do this?" **Bobo Doll Study (1961):** → Children watched adult hit inflatable doll → Children imitated aggressive behavior → Especially when model was REWARDED **Reciprocal Determinism:** → Person ↔ Behavior ↔ Environment (all influence each other) → Not just environment shaping us — WE shape it too

memoryAids.title: Multicultural Counseling Competencies
memoryAids.content: **Sue et al. — 3 domains × 3 dimensions:** 📊 **3 Domains (what to be competent in):** 🪞 **1. Awareness** of own assumptions/biases → Understand your own cultural conditioning → Recognize your privileges and blind spots → "What lens am I looking through?" 📚 **2. Knowledge** of client's worldview → Learn about different cultural groups → Understand systemic oppression, racism, microaggressions → Know culturally-specific models of mental health 🛠️ **3. Skills** to intervene appropriately → Adapt techniques to cultural context → Use culturally appropriate assessment → Seek consultation when working across cultures **Key concepts:** → **Emic** = Culture-SPECIFIC approach (inside the culture) → **Etic** = Universal approach (across cultures) → **Cultural encapsulation** (Wrenn): assuming own worldview is universal → **Microaggressions** (Sue): subtle, unintentional slights → **Racial identity models** (Cross, Helms): stages of identity development **EPPP tip:** Best answer is usually most culturally sensitive option

memoryAids.title: Cognitive Biases in Clinical Judgment
memoryAids.content: **7 biases that lead clinicians astray:** 🔨 **Confirmation bias** = Seek info that CONFIRMS your hypothesis → Ignore contradictory evidence → "I think it's depression" → only ask depression questions ⚓ **Anchoring** = Over-rely on FIRST piece of information → Initial impression dominates even with new data → First diagnosis tends to stick 📊 **Base rate neglect** = Ignore prevalence data → Rare disorder seems likely because symptoms match → But the base rate is very low! 🧠 **Availability heuristic** = Judge by what comes to MIND easily → Recent dramatic case → overestimate that condition → Just saw a BPD case → see BPD everywhere 👤 **Fundamental attribution error** = Blame the PERSON, ignore the SITUATION → "They're non-compliant" vs "Their barriers are too high" 🔄 **Hindsight bias** = "I knew it all along" → After outcome is known, overestimate predictability 📏 **Illusory correlation** = See patterns that DON'T exist → "All my depressed clients are creative" **Cure:** Use structured assessment, consider base rates, seek disconfirming evidence

memoryAids.title: Marcia's Identity Statuses
memoryAids.content: **James Marcia — Extension of Erikson's identity crisis:** 📊 **2 dimensions:** Exploration (crisis) × Commitment | | Low Commitment | High Commitment | |---|---|---| | **Low Exploration** | 😶 Diffusion | 📋 Foreclosure | | **High Exploration** | 🤔 Moratorium | ✅ Achievement | 😶 **Identity Diffusion:** → No crisis, no commitment → "I don't know and I don't care" → Apathetic, directionless 📋 **Identity Foreclosure:** → No crisis, BUT committed (adopted others' values) → "My parents want me to be a doctor, so I will" → Never explored alternatives 🤔 **Identity Moratorium:** → Currently IN crisis, exploring → "I'm trying to figure out who I am" → Active experimentation, anxiety is normal ✅ **Identity Achievement:** → Explored AND committed → "I tried different things and chose what fits ME" → Most mature status **Key:** These are STATUSES, not stages — people can move between them Most common in adolescence but also occurs in emerging adulthood

memoryAids.title: Temperament — Thomas & Chess
memoryAids.content: **"EDS" — 3 temperament types from New York Longitudinal Study:** 😊 **E**asy Child (~40%): → Regular biological rhythms (eating, sleeping) → Positive mood, adaptable to change → Approaches new situations with interest → Low-intensity reactions 😤 **D**ifficult Child (~10%): → Irregular rhythms → Negative mood, slow to adapt → Withdraws from new situations → Intense emotional reactions → Higher risk for behavior problems IF poor fit 🐢 **S**low-to-Warm-Up (~15%): → Initially cautious/withdrawn → Mild reactions, gradual adaptation → "Give me time and I'll come around" → Low activity level ❓ **35%** don't fit neatly into any category 🔑 **Goodness of Fit** (KEY CONCEPT): → Match between child's temperament and environment → Difficult child + patient parent = GOOD outcome → Difficult child + rigid parent = POOR outcome → It's the FIT that matters, not the temperament alone! **9 dimensions:** Activity, Rhythmicity, Approach, Adaptability, Intensity, Mood, Persistence, Distractibility, Threshold

memoryAids.title: Motivational Interviewing
memoryAids.content: **Miller & Rollnick — "OARS" + Spirit:** 🧠 **Spirit of MI** (foundational mindset): → **Partnership** — collaborative, not expert-driven → **Acceptance** — respect autonomy, affirm strengths → **Compassion** — prioritize client's welfare → **Evocation** — draw out client's own motivations 🎣 **OARS** — Core skills: → **O**pen-ended questions: "What concerns you about your drinking?" → **A**ffirmations: "It took courage to come here" → **R**eflective listening: Mirror back what you hear (simple + complex) → **S**ummaries: Gather themes together 🔄 **Stages of Change** (Prochaska & DiClemente): → Precontemplation → Contemplation → Preparation → Action → Maintenance → (Relapse can occur at any stage) 💬 **Change Talk** vs **Sustain Talk:** → Change talk: reasons FOR change (reinforce this!) → Sustain talk: reasons AGAINST change (roll with it, don't argue!) ⚠️ **"Rolling with resistance"** — Don't argue! → Resistance = signal to change your approach → Arguing = "righting reflex" (counterproductive)

memoryAids.title: Rogers' Core Conditions
memoryAids.content: **Carl Rogers — Person-Centered Therapy's 3 essential conditions:** 💕 **Unconditional Positive Regard (UPR):** → Accept the client COMPLETELY — no conditions of worth → Separate the person from the behavior → "I value you as a person, even if I don't approve of everything you do" → Conditions of worth = "I'll love you IF..." → incongruence 🪞 **Empathic Understanding:** → See the world through the CLIENT'S eyes → "Walk a mile in their shoes" → Communicate understanding back to the client → Not sympathy (feeling FOR them) — empathy (feeling WITH them) 🎯 **Congruence / Genuineness:** → Therapist is REAL, authentic, transparent → No hiding behind professional facade → What therapist feels = what they express → Most important condition (according to Rogers) **Key concepts:** → **Self-actualization** = Innate drive toward growth → **Incongruence** = Gap between real self and ideal self → distress → **Non-directive** → later called "person-centered" → Rogers rejected diagnosis, interpretation, advice-giving → The relationship IS the therapy

memoryAids.title: Paradoxical Interventions
memoryAids.content: **"Prescribing the symptom" — When telling someone to DO the problem works:** 🔄 **How it works:** → Therapist instructs client to deliberately DO the symptom → Client gains sense of CONTROL over it → Symptom loses its power when done intentionally 📋 **Examples:** → Insomnia: "Try to stay awake as long as possible" → Anxiety: "Try to make yourself as anxious as you can" → Arguing couple: "I want you to have your argument, but schedule it for 8pm" 🏠 **Used in:** → **Strategic Family Therapy** (Haley, Madanes) → Therapist is directive, plans interventions → Reframing: change the MEANING of the behavior → **Milan Systemic** (Selvini Palazzoli) → Circular questioning, positive connotation → Long brief therapy (monthly sessions) → **Logotherapy** (Frankl) → "Paradoxical intention" — humor to defuse anxiety → "Dereflection" — redirect attention away from symptom **Caution:** Not appropriate for all clients (psychotic, suicidal) → Use only when client can understand the rationale

memoryAids.title: ANOVA Assumptions & Post-Hocs
memoryAids.content: **"HIN" — 3 assumptions of ANOVA:** 📊 **H**omogeneity of variance (homoscedasticity) → Groups have EQUAL variances → Test with Levene's test → Violation: use Welch's ANOVA 📈 **I**ndependence of observations → Scores in one group don't affect scores in another → Random assignment helps ensure this → Violation: use repeated-measures ANOVA 🔔 **N**ormal distribution → DV is normally distributed within each group → ANOVA is ROBUST to mild violations (especially with large N) **When ANOVA is significant → Post-hoc tests:** → **Tukey's HSD** = Most common (controls Type I error well) → **Scheffé** = Most conservative (fewest false positives) → **Bonferroni** = Simple correction (α / number of comparisons) → **Fisher's LSD** = Least conservative (most liberal) **Planned vs Post-hoc:** → A priori (planned) contrasts: Decided BEFORE data collection → Post-hoc: Decided AFTER seeing significant F-test → Planned comparisons are MORE powerful

memoryAids.title: Regression to the Mean
memoryAids.content: **The most misunderstood statistical concept:** 📊 **What it is:** → Extreme scores tend to move TOWARD the average on retesting → NOT because of real change — just statistical probability! 📏 **Examples:** → Student scores 95 on test 1 → likely scores LOWER on test 2 → Student scores 55 on test 1 → likely scores HIGHER on test 2 → Patient at WORST symptoms → likely improves even without treatment ⚠️ **Why it matters for research:** → Placebo effect may partly be regression to the mean → Selecting extreme groups → they'll look like they "improved" → Without control group, can't distinguish real effect from regression 🛡️ **How to handle it:** → Use CONTROL groups (they also regress!) → Use multiple measurements (not just one pretest) → Don't select participants based on extreme scores **Key EPPP scenario:** → "Patients with the most severe symptoms showed the most improvement" → Red flag! Could be regression to the mean, not treatment effect → Need a control group to make causal claims

memoryAids.title: Tarasoff Ruling — Complete
memoryAids.content: **The most important legal case in psychology:** ⚖️ **Tarasoff v. Regents (1976) — California Supreme Court** **The case:** → Prosenjit Poddar told therapist he planned to kill Tatiana Tarasoff → Therapist warned campus police (who detained & released Poddar) → Therapist did NOT warn Tatiana or her family → Poddar killed Tatiana 📋 **Tarasoff I (1974):** Duty to WARN → Must warn the intended victim directly 📋 **Tarasoff II (1976):** Duty to PROTECT (broader) → Must take REASONABLE steps to protect → Options: warn victim, notify police, hospitalize client → Professional judgment about WHICH action 🔑 **When Tarasoff applies:** → Identifiable potential victim(s) → Serious threat of physical violence → Therapist has reason to believe threat is credible 🔑 **When it does NOT apply:** → General dangerousness (no specific victim) → Property damage threats → Self-harm (different duty — duty to prevent suicide) **Key:** Not all states follow Tarasoff! → Some have NO duty to warn/protect → Some are mandatory; some are permissive → Know YOUR state's law!

memoryAids.title: Record Keeping & Retention
memoryAids.content: **Record keeping supports continuity, accountability, billing, and legal/ethical compliance.** Include clinically necessary identifying information, consent and role clarification, assessment or treatment rationale, services and progress, risk decisions, consultations, billing, and disposition as applicable. ⏰ **Retention is jurisdiction- and setting-specific.** APA record-keeping guidance has offered general time frames, but statutes, regulations, payer rules, contracts, institutional policy, litigation holds, and rules for minors can require different periods. Verify current applicable requirements rather than memorizing one universal number. 🔒 Protect confidentiality, access, integrity, secure disposal, and continuity if the psychologist becomes unavailable. A subpoena requires timely lawful response and privilege analysis; it does not always authorize immediate disclosure.
memoryAids.reviewNote: Removed universal retention periods and distinguished guidance from jurisdictional, institutional, payer, and litigation requirements.

memoryAids.title: Classical Conditioning Phenomena
memoryAids.content: **Beyond basic Pavlov — Advanced CC concepts:** 🔔 **Acquisition** = Learning the CS-US association → CS (bell) + US (food) → UR (salivation) → After pairing: CS alone → CR (salivation) ❌ **Extinction** = CS presented WITHOUT US → CR decreases → NOT unlearning! The association is still there 🔄 **Spontaneous Recovery** = Extinguished CR returns after rest → Proves extinction ≠ forgetting 📊 **Stimulus Generalization** = Similar stimuli → similar response → Little Albert: white rat → white rabbit, fur coat, Santa mask → Gradient: more similar = stronger response 🎯 **Stimulus Discrimination** = Respond to CS but NOT similar stimuli → Learn that ONLY the specific bell predicts food 🔗 **Higher-Order Conditioning:** → CS1 (bell) paired with US (food) → CR → CS2 (light) paired with CS1 (bell) → CR → Light alone now triggers salivation! ⏰ **Timing matters:** → Delay conditioning (best): CS starts before US, overlaps → Trace: CS ends before US starts (gap = harder) → Simultaneous: same time (poor learning) → Backward: US before CS (worst/no learning)

memoryAids.title: Memory Systems Taxonomy
memoryAids.content: **"EPP-SS" — The complete memory map:** 🧠 **Long-Term Memory** splits into TWO systems: 📖 **Explicit (Declarative)** = Conscious, "knowing THAT" → **Episodic** = Personal events ("my wedding day") → Tied to time and place → Tulving's concept → **Semantic** = General knowledge ("Paris is in France") → Facts, concepts, language → Not tied to personal experience 🔧 **Implicit (Non-Declarative)** = Unconscious, "knowing HOW" → **Procedural** = Skills & habits (riding a bike, typing) → Cerebellum + basal ganglia → **Priming** = Exposure facilitates later processing → See "doctor" → faster to recognize "nurse" → **Conditioning** = Learned associations 🧪 **Key brain structures:** → Hippocampus = Explicit memory FORMATION (not storage) → H.M. (Henry Molaison): removed hippocampi → can't form new explicit memories → BUT could still learn new procedures (implicit intact!) → Amygdala = Emotional memories → Cerebellum = Procedural/motor memories **Amnesia types:** → Anterograde: Can't form NEW memories (hippocampus) → Retrograde: Can't recall OLD memories

memoryAids.title: Substance Use Disorder — DSM-5
memoryAids.content: **DSM-5 combined abuse + dependence into ONE diagnosis:** 📊 **11 criteria (need 2+ in 12 months):** 🔄 **Impaired Control (4):** → 1. Take more than intended → 2. Can't cut down (persistent desire) → 3. Lots of time obtaining/using/recovering → 4. Craving or strong urge to use 🏠 **Social Impairment (3):** → 5. Fails to fulfill role obligations (work, school, home) → 6. Continued use despite social problems → 7. Gives up important activities ⚠️ **Risky Use (2):** → 8. Use in physically hazardous situations → 9. Continued despite physical/psychological problems 💊 **Pharmacological (2):** → 10. Tolerance (need more for same effect) → 11. Withdrawal (symptoms when stopping) 📊 **Severity:** → Mild: 2-3 criteria → Moderate: 4-5 criteria → Severe: 6+ criteria **Key change from DSM-IV:** → No more separate "abuse" vs "dependence" → "Legal problems" criterion removed → "Craving" criterion added

memoryAids.title: Eating Disorders Comparison
memoryAids.content: **DSM-5 — 3 main eating disorders side by side:** 🦴 **Anorexia Nervosa:** → Restriction of intake → significantly low body weight → Intense FEAR of gaining weight → Distorted body image ("I'm fat" when underweight) → Types: Restricting vs Binge-purge → Highest mortality rate of any mental disorder! → Medical: amenorrhea, lanugo, bradycardia, osteoporosis 🔄 **Bulimia Nervosa:** → Binge eating + compensatory behaviors (purging, laxatives, exercise) → At least 1x/week for 3 months → Usually NORMAL weight → Russell's sign (knuckle calluses from purging) → Dental erosion, electrolyte imbalance → Self-worth overly influenced by body shape/weight 🍔 **Binge Eating Disorder (NEW in DSM-5):** → Binge eating WITHOUT compensatory behaviors → Eating rapidly, until uncomfortably full → Eating alone (embarrassment) → Marked distress about binging → At least 1x/week for 3 months → Often overweight/obese → Most common eating disorder **Key:** Anorexia = underweight; Bulimia = normal weight; BED = often overweight

memoryAids.title: Mood Disorders Deep Dive
memoryAids.content: **MDD vs Bipolar I vs Bipolar II vs Cyclothymia:** 😔 **Major Depressive Disorder (MDD):** → 5+ symptoms for 2+ weeks → MUST include depressed mood OR anhedonia → SIG E CAPS: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidality → No history of mania or hypomania 🎢 **Bipolar I:** → At least ONE manic episode (7+ days or hospitalization) → Mania: grandiosity, decreased sleep, pressured speech, racing thoughts, risky behavior → May or may not have depression → DIG FAST: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity increase, Sleep decrease, Talkativeness 🎭 **Bipolar II:** → At least ONE hypomanic episode (4+ days) + ONE major depressive episode → Hypomania = less severe mania (no hospitalization, no psychosis) → Often misdiagnosed as MDD (ask about hypomania!) 🔄 **Cyclothymic Disorder:** → Chronic fluctuating mood: hypomanic + depressive symptoms → 2+ years (never symptom-free for >2 months) → Never meets full criteria for mania or MDD **Key:** Bipolar I does NOT require depression; Bipolar II DOES

memoryAids.title: Competency to Stand Trial
memoryAids.content: **Dusky v. United States (1960) — THE standard:** ⚖️ **Two-prong test:** → 1. **Factual understanding** of proceedings → Understands charges, roles (judge, jury, attorney) → Knows possible penalties → 2. **Rational ability** to consult with attorney → Can communicate, make decisions → Can assist in own defense rationally 📋 **Key distinctions:** → Competency = PRESENT mental state (can they stand trial NOW?) → Insanity = PAST mental state (were they sane DURING the crime?) → Competency is MORE commonly raised than insanity 🧠 **Assessment tools:** → MacCAT-CA (MacArthur Competence Assessment Tool) → GCCT (Georgia Court Competency Test) → CST (Competency Screening Test) ❓ **If found incompetent:** → Trial is POSTPONED (not dismissed) → Treatment to restore competency (usually medication) → Jackson v. Indiana: can't be held indefinitely → If can't be restored → civil commitment possible **Key:** Low threshold — most defendants ARE competent → Even intellectually disabled or mentally ill CAN be competent

memoryAids.title: Endocrine System & Hormones
memoryAids.content: **Key hormones psychologists need to know:** 🧠 **Hypothalamus** = Master controller → Controls pituitary via releasing hormones → Links nervous system to endocrine system 🎯 **Pituitary Gland** = "Master gland" → Anterior: Growth hormone, ACTH, TSH, FSH/LH, prolactin → Posterior: Oxytocin, ADH (vasopressin) 🦋 **Thyroid** = Metabolism → Too much (hyperthyroid): anxiety, weight loss, agitation → Too little (hypothyroid): depression, fatigue, weight gain → Always rule out thyroid before diagnosing depression! 🔺 **Adrenal Glands:** → Cortex: Cortisol (stress), aldosterone → Medulla: Epinephrine (adrenaline), norepinephrine → Cortisol follows circadian rhythm (peak in morning) 💕 **Oxytocin** = Bonding hormone → Released during childbirth, breastfeeding, physical touch → Increases trust and social bonding 🌙 **Melatonin** (pineal) = Sleep-wake cycle **Key EPPP connection:** Many psychological symptoms have endocrine causes → Always consider medical/biological rule-outs first!

memoryAids.title: Aging & Late Adulthood
memoryAids.content: **Key concepts for gerontological psychology:** 🧠 **Cognitive changes with aging:** → **Fluid intelligence** DECLINES (processing speed, novel problem-solving) → **Crystallized intelligence** STABLE or INCREASES (knowledge, vocabulary) → Wisdom may increase with age (Baltes) → "Use it or lose it" — cognitive engagement protects 💪 **Successful aging theories:** → **Activity Theory**: Stay active → age well → **Disengagement Theory** (Cumming & Henry): Natural withdrawal → Controversial — not supported well → **Socioemotional Selectivity** (Carstensen): → Older adults prioritize EMOTIONAL goals → Smaller but closer social networks → "Positivity effect" — focus more on positive memories 🧩 **Neurocognitive Disorders (DSM-5):** → Mild NCD = mild deficits, independence maintained → Major NCD = significant deficits, impaired independence → Types: Alzheimer's (most common, 60-80%), Vascular, Lewy Body, Frontotemporal **Kübler-Ross 5 stages of grief:** DABDA → Denial → Anger → Bargaining → Depression → Acceptance → Not everyone goes through all stages or in this order!

memoryAids.title: Existential Therapy
memoryAids.content: **"DFIM" — 4 ultimate concerns (Yalom):** 💀 **D**eath = We all die → anxiety about mortality → Death awareness can motivate authentic living → "Staring into the sun" — confronting mortality 🕊️ **F**reedom = Total responsibility for our choices → No one else to blame → existential anxiety → Freedom is both liberating AND terrifying → "Condemned to be free" (Sartre) 🏝️ **I**solation = Ultimately alone in our experience → Existential isolation ≠ loneliness → No one can fully share your subjective experience → Yet we long for connection ❓ **M**eaninglessness = Life has no inherent meaning → We must CREATE our own meaning → Frankl: "Man's Search for Meaning" → Logotherapy: Find purpose through values, creativity, suffering **Key existential therapists:** → **Yalom** = 4 ultimate concerns, group therapy → **Frankl** = Logotherapy, meaning through suffering → **May** = Anxiety as growth opportunity → **Bugental** = Search for authenticity **Existential therapy is NOT technique-driven** → Focus on the therapeutic RELATIONSHIP and honest dialogue

memoryAids.title: Structural Family Therapy — Minuchin
memoryAids.content: **Salvador Minuchin — Focus on family STRUCTURE:** 🏗️ **Core concept:** Family problems = structural problems → Fix the STRUCTURE → fix the symptoms 📊 **Key terms:** → **Subsystems**: Spousal, parental, sibling → **Boundaries**: Rules about who participates how → **Enmeshed** = Too close (no autonomy, over-involvement) → **Disengaged** = Too distant (no support, disconnected) → **Clear** = Healthy balance (GOAL of therapy) → **Hierarchy**: Parents should be IN CHARGE (not kids!) 🛠️ **Techniques:** → **Joining** = Enter the family system, build rapport → Mimesis: mirror family's style → Tracking: follow their communication patterns → **Enactment** = Have family act out problem IN session → "Show me, don't tell me" → **Restructuring** = Change boundaries and hierarchies → Strengthen parental subsystem → Create appropriate generational boundaries → **Unbalancing** = Temporarily side with one family member **Key:** Minuchin worked extensively with low-SES families → Developed approach at Philadelphia Child Guidance Center → Originally studied anorexia ("psychosomatic families")

memoryAids.title: Gestalt Therapy Techniques
memoryAids.content: **Fritz Perls — "Here and now" awareness:** 🎯 **Core philosophy:** → Focus on PRESENT experience, not past → "How are you feeling RIGHT NOW?" → Awareness itself is curative → Unfinished business = past issues blocking present awareness 🪑 **Empty Chair Technique:** → Client talks TO an empty chair → Chair represents: absent person, part of self, feeling → Then SWITCHES chairs and responds → Resolves unfinished business, integrates conflicting parts 👤 **"I" Language:** → Change "you" or "it" to "I" → "You get angry when..." → "I get angry when..." → Takes ownership of feelings 🎭 **Exaggeration:** → Exaggerate a gesture or statement to amplify awareness → "Make that fist even tighter — what does it want to do?" 🔄 **Reversal:** → Act out the OPPOSITE of what you typically do → Shy person practices being assertive → Reveals hidden aspects of personality **Key Gestalt concepts:** → **Figure-ground** in perception of experience → **Contact boundary disturbances**: introjection, projection, retroflection, confluence → Topdog vs Underdog (internal conflict) → "Lose your mind and come to your senses"

memoryAids.title: Common Factors in Therapy
memoryAids.content: **Why ALL therapies seem to work — "The Dodo Bird Verdict":** 🐦 **Dodo Bird Verdict** (Luborsky, 1975): → "Everyone has won, and all shall have prizes" → Different therapies produce SIMILAR outcomes → Why? Because they share COMMON FACTORS 📊 **What predicts therapy outcome?** → 40% Client/extratherapeutic factors (motivation, social support) → 30% Therapeutic relationship (alliance!) → 15% Expectancy/placebo (hope, belief in treatment) → 15% Model/technique (specific interventions) → (Lambert's pie chart — percentages are approximate) 🤝 **Therapeutic Alliance** (Bordin): → 3 components: Bond + Goals + Tasks → BEST predictor of outcome across ALL therapies → Early alliance (by session 3) predicts final outcome → Alliance ruptures → repair them → can strengthen treatment 📋 **Evidence-Based Practice (EBP) = 3 components:** → 1. Best available research evidence → 2. Clinical expertise → 3. Patient values/preferences → All THREE matter — not just research!

memoryAids.title: Threats to Internal Validity
memoryAids.content: **"THIS MAID" — 8 threats that ruin your experiment:** 📊 **T**esting = Taking a test the first time improves the second score → Practice effect, familiarity with items 📈 **H**istory = External events during the study affect results → Natural disaster occurs between pretest and posttest 📐 **I**nstrumentation = Measurement tool changes → Observers become better/worse over time → Equipment calibration drifts 📉 **S**election = Groups differ at the START → Non-random assignment → pre-existing differences → Solution: random assignment or matching 🌱 **M**aturation = Natural changes over time → Participants grow older, tired, wiser during study 📊 **A**ttrition (Mortality) = Participants DROP OUT → Especially problematic if dropout is non-random → Treatment group loses discouraged members 🔄 **I**nteraction effects = Selection × maturation, etc. → Groups mature at different RATES 📏 **D**iffusion = Treatment "leaks" to control group → Control group learns about the treatment **Plus:** Statistical regression (extreme scores regress to mean) **Solution:** Random assignment + control group addresses MOST threats

memoryAids.title: Nonparametric Tests
memoryAids.content: **When you can't use parametric tests:** 📊 **When to use nonparametric:** → Data is ordinal or nominal (not interval/ratio) → Assumptions violated (non-normal, unequal variances) → Small sample size → Outliers that skew results 🔄 **Nonparametric equivalents:** | Parametric | Nonparametric | |---|---| | Independent t-test | **Mann-Whitney U** | | Paired t-test | **Wilcoxon signed-rank** | | One-way ANOVA | **Kruskal-Wallis H** | | Repeated ANOVA | **Friedman test** | | Pearson r | **Spearman rho** | | Chi-square | (already nonparametric!) | 📋 **Chi-Square tests:** → **Goodness of fit** = Does observed match expected? (1 variable) → **Test of independence** = Are 2 categorical variables related? (2 variables) → Uses FREQUENCY data (counts, not means) **Trade-off:** Nonparametric tests are LESS powerful (more Type II errors) → But they're more ROBUST (work with messy data) → Use parametric when assumptions are met!

memoryAids.title: Child Abuse Reporting
memoryAids.content: **Mandated reporting — NO exceptions for confidentiality:** ⚠️ **Who must report?** → Psychologists are MANDATORY reporters in all 50 states → Report to: Child Protective Services (CPS) or law enforcement → Immunity from liability when reporting in good faith 📋 **When to report:** → REASONABLE SUSPICION (not certainty!) → You do NOT need to investigate or confirm → You do NOT need proof → Low threshold: "I have reason to believe..." 🚫 **What NOT to do:** → Don't investigate yourself (that's CPS's job) → Don't contact the alleged perpetrator → Don't delay reporting to "gather more information" → Don't ask the child leading questions ⏰ **Timing:** → Report IMMEDIATELY (or within 24-48 hours, varies by state) → Typically: phone call FIRST, written report follows 📊 **Types of abuse to report:** → Physical abuse → Sexual abuse → Emotional/psychological abuse → Neglect (most common type!) **Key:** Failure to report = legal liability + ethical violation → Even if client confides abuse in therapy → Confidentiality does NOT protect abusers

memoryAids.title: End-of-Life Ethical Issues
memoryAids.content: **Key ethical concepts for terminally ill clients:** 🏥 **Advance Directives:** → Living will = states treatment preferences → Durable power of attorney (healthcare proxy) → Complete BEFORE incapacitation → Psychologist can help with decision-making process 💊 **Assisted dying/Euthanasia:** → APA has no official position (leaves to individual ethics) → Legal in some states (Oregon Death with Dignity Act, 1997) → Psychologist's role: assess mental competency → NOT the psychologist's role: make the decision 🧠 **Competency assessment:** → Can the client understand the information? → Can they appreciate how it applies to them? → Can they reason about options? → Can they communicate a stable choice? 📋 **Psychologist's role with dying clients:** → Help process grief, fear, meaning-making → Support family members → Pain management consultation → Do NOT abandon dying clients (ethical violation!) → Continue treatment as long as client benefits **Kübler-Ross reminder:** Stages are NOT prescriptive → Don't force clients through stages → Honor their unique grieving process

memoryAids.title: ADHD — DSM-5 Presentations
memoryAids.content: **ADHD presentations:** predominantly inattentive, predominantly hyperactive/impulsive, or combined. Diagnosis requires a persistent developmentally inappropriate symptom pattern, several symptoms before age 12, presence in two or more settings, impairment, and consideration of alternative explanations. Thresholds differ for older adolescents/adults. Treatment is individualized and can include behavioral parent/classroom interventions, organizational and skills supports, environmental accommodations, stimulant or nonstimulant medication, and coordinated care. Medication does not “paradoxically calm every hyperactive child,” and no single combination is automatically best for every person.
memoryAids.reviewNote: Removed paradoxical-calming and universally-best-treatment claims; added differential assessment and individualized multimodal care.

memoryAids.title: Autism Spectrum Disorder
memoryAids.content: **Autism spectrum disorder — two core DSM-5-TR domains:** 1. Persistent differences or deficits in social communication and social interaction across contexts. 2. Restricted or repetitive behavior, interests, or activities, including sensory features (at least two types). Features begin in the early developmental period, cause clinically significant impairment, and are not better explained by intellectual disability alone. Specify language, intellectual, medical/genetic, catatonia, and support-level features as appropriate. Population surveillance estimates change by year, methods, location, age, sex, race/ethnicity, and access to identification. Avoid memorizing one prevalence figure as timeless or treating support levels as fixed descriptions of a person's worth or potential.
memoryAids.reviewNote: Removed stale prevalence and deterministic support-level language; retained current surveillance context and diagnostic-domain distinctions.

memoryAids.title: Intellectual Disability
memoryAids.content: **DSM-5-TR intellectual disability requires all three:** 1. Deficits in intellectual functions confirmed by clinical assessment and appropriately administered testing. 2. Deficits in adaptive functioning that limit independence and social responsibility across conceptual, social, or practical domains. 3. Onset during the developmental period. Severity is based primarily on adaptive functioning and support needs, not a fixed IQ band or assumed school grade. Test scores include measurement error and must be interpreted with language, culture, education, sensory/motor status, and opportunity. Avoid outdated labels and deterministic claims about work or independent living.
memoryAids.reviewNote: Removed obsolete IQ severity bands and assumed school/work outcomes; emphasized adaptive functioning, context, measurement error, and supports.

memoryAids.title: Dissociative Disorders
memoryAids.content: **"DAD" — 3 main dissociative disorders:** 👤 **D**issociative Identity Disorder (DID): → 2+ distinct personality states ("alters") → Gaps in memory for events, personal info → NOT the same as schizophrenia! → Strongly associated with severe childhood trauma → Most controversial diagnosis in psychology → "Host" personality may be unaware of alters 😶 **D**issociative Amnesia: → Inability to recall important personal information → Usually related to trauma or stress → Types: Localized (specific time), Selective, Generalized → **Dissociative Fugue** = Amnesia + unexpected travel + new identity → Now a specifier, not separate diagnosis 🌫️ **D**epersonalization/Derealization Disorder: → Depersonalization: feel detached from SELF ("watching myself") → Derealization: world feels UNREAL ("this isn't real") → Reality testing remains INTACT (they KNOW it's not real) → Most common dissociative experience **Key:** All involve disruption of consciousness, memory, identity, or perception → Dissociation exists on a continuum (daydreaming → DID) → Dissociation = defense against overwhelming trauma

memoryAids.title: Insanity Defense Standards
memoryAids.content: **4 insanity standards — from most to least common:** 📋 **M'Naghten Rule** (1843) — Most widely used: → "Did not KNOW the nature and quality of the act" → OR "Did not know it was WRONG" → Purely COGNITIVE test (did they understand?) → Does NOT consider inability to control behavior 📋 **Irresistible Impulse Test** (added to M'Naghten): → "Could not CONTROL their behavior" → Even if they KNEW it was wrong → The "policeman at the elbow" test → Addresses volitional component M'Naghten misses 📋 **ALI/Model Penal Code** (1962): → Lacks "substantial capacity" to appreciate wrongfulness → OR conform conduct to the law → BOTH cognitive AND volitional → Was broadest standard, used by many federal courts 📋 **Durham Rule** (1954) — Rarely used: → Act was "product of mental disease or defect" → Broadest and most criticized standard → Only used in New Hampshire now **Post-Hinckley (1982):** Federal law narrowed to cognitive only → GBMI (Guilty But Mentally Ill) = alternative in some states → Insanity defense is RARELY raised (<1%) and RARELY successful

memoryAids.title: Theories of Emotion
memoryAids.content: **"Which comes first — feeling or body reaction?"** 🏃 **James-Lange Theory:** → Event → Body reaction FIRST → Then emotion → "I'm running, therefore I must be afraid" → "We feel sorry because we cry" (James) → Different emotions = different physiological patterns → Problem: What about spinal cord injuries? (Still feel emotions!) 🧠 **Cannon-Bard Theory:** → Event → Body AND emotion happen SIMULTANEOUSLY → Thalamus sends signals to BOTH cortex and body at same time → Body reaction doesn't cause the emotion → Problem: Too simplistic about the thalamus's role 🏷️ **Schachter-Singer (Two-Factor) Theory:** → Event → Arousal + COGNITIVE LABEL → Emotion → Need BOTH physiological arousal AND interpretation → "I'm aroused AND I'm at a party = I must be happy" → Misattribution of arousal: wrong label = wrong emotion → Bridge study: Shaky bridge → attractive interviewer → "attraction" 😊 **Lazarus (Cognitive Appraisal):** → Cognition (appraisal) comes FIRST, THEN emotion → How you INTERPRET the event determines the emotion → Reappraisal can change emotion **Zajonc:** Emotion can occur WITHOUT cognition (mere exposure effect)

memoryAids.title: Intelligence Theories
memoryAids.content: **Major theories — from g to multiple intelligences:** 🧠 **Spearman — g factor:** → General intelligence (g) underlies ALL cognitive abilities → Plus specific factors (s) for each task → Factor analysis revealed g 📊 **Cattell-Horn (Gf-Gc):** → **Fluid (Gf)** = Novel problem-solving, pattern recognition → Declines with age → Culture-free (less influenced by education) → **Crystallized (Gc)** = Accumulated knowledge, vocabulary → Stable or increases with age → Strongly influenced by education/culture 🧩 **Gardner — Multiple Intelligences (8+):** → Linguistic, Logical-mathematical, Spatial → Musical, Bodily-kinesthetic, Interpersonal → Intrapersonal, Naturalist (+ possibly Existential) → Criticism: More like talents than "intelligence" 🔺 **Sternberg — Triarchic Theory (3):** → **Analytical** = Academic, problem-solving (traditional IQ) → **Creative** = Novel situations, insight → **Practical** = "Street smarts," adapting to environment → Later: WICS model (Wisdom + Intelligence + Creativity + Synthesized) 📊 **CHC Theory** (Cattell-Horn-Carroll) = Modern consensus → Combines g + broad abilities + narrow abilities

memoryAids.title: Cognitive Distortions — Beck
memoryAids.content: **Aaron Beck's common thinking errors:** ⬛ **All-or-Nothing Thinking** = Black & white, no gray area → "If I'm not perfect, I'm a complete failure" 🔮 **Fortune Telling** = Predicting negative outcomes → "I know the interview will go badly" 🧠 **Mind Reading** = Assuming you know what others think → "She thinks I'm stupid" 📏 **Magnification/Minimization** = Blowing up negatives, shrinking positives → "That mistake was CATASTROPHIC" + "My A grade was just luck" 🏷️ **Labeling** = Attaching a global label from one event → "I failed the test → I AM a failure" 👤 **Personalization** = Blaming yourself for external events → "It rained at the picnic — it's MY fault" 🔍 **Mental Filter** = Focusing on ONE negative detail → 99 compliments + 1 criticism → dwell on the criticism 📊 **Overgeneralization** = One event → "this ALWAYS happens" → "I got rejected → nobody will EVER love me" 🎭 **Emotional Reasoning** = "I feel it, so it must be true" → "I feel anxious, so something bad must be happening" 📋 **"Should" Statements** = Rigid rules about how things must be → "I SHOULD be able to handle this" → guilt, frustration **Therapy:** Identify → Challenge → Replace with balanced thoughts

memoryAids.title: Research Ethics — Belmont Report
memoryAids.content: **The Belmont Report (1979) — 3 core principles:** 🤝 **Respect for Persons:** → Individuals are autonomous agents → Vulnerable populations deserve extra protection → Application: INFORMED CONSENT → Voluntary, adequate information, comprehension ⚖️ **Beneficence:** → Maximize benefits, minimize harm → "Do no harm" + actively seek good → Application: RISK-BENEFIT ASSESSMENT → Benefits must outweigh risks → Risks must be minimized 🏛️ **Justice:** → Fair distribution of research burdens and benefits → Don't exploit vulnerable populations → Application: FAIR SELECTION of subjects → Tuskegee study = classic violation (used disadvantaged group) 📋 **Additional research protections:** → **IRB** (Institutional Review Board) = Must approve research → **Deception** = Only if justified AND debriefed afterward → **Debriefing** = Explain true purpose after study → **Right to withdraw** = Can quit at any time without penalty → **Confidentiality** = Protect participant data **Key cases:** Tuskegee, Milgram, Stanford Prison, Little Albert → All raised ethical concerns → led to modern protections

memoryAids.title: Test Bias & Fairness
memoryAids.content: **Types of bias in psychological testing:** 📊 **Construct Bias:** → Test measures DIFFERENT constructs in different groups → An IQ test measuring knowledge vs reasoning ability → across cultures 📏 **Method Bias:** → Differences in HOW the test is administered → Unfamiliar format, language barriers → Test-taking style differences (e.g., speed vs accuracy orientation) 📋 **Item Bias (DIF):** → Specific items function differently across groups → Differential Item Functioning: same ability → different answers → A vocabulary item using culturally specific content ⚖️ **Predictive Bias:** → Test predicts outcomes DIFFERENTLY for different groups → If GRE predicts grad school success for Group A but not Group B → Slope/intercept differences in regression lines 🛡️ **Addressing bias:** → Culture-fair tests (Raven's Progressive Matrices) → Translation + back-translation for other languages → Norming on diverse, representative samples → DIF analysis to identify biased items → Use MULTIPLE assessment methods (not just one test) **Key EPPP fact:** Tests can be reliable but NOT valid for certain populations

memoryAids.title: Performance Appraisal Errors
memoryAids.content: **"CHALRS" — 6 common rater errors:** 😇 **C**entral Tendency = Rate everyone as AVERAGE → Avoid extreme ratings (play it safe) → Everyone gets "meets expectations" 😊 **H**alo Effect = One positive trait → rate everything positive → "She's great at presenting → she must be great at everything" → Opposite: **Horns Effect** (one negative → everything negative) 📊 **A**djust to yourself = Compare employees to YOURSELF → "I would have done it differently" = low rating → Standards based on rater's own performance 🕐 **L**eniency / Severity: → Leniency: Rate everyone too HIGH → Severity: Rate everyone too LOW → Both reduce discrimination between employees ⏰ **R**ecency Effect = Overweight RECENT performance → Last month matters more than the whole year → Solution: keep ongoing records 🔍 **S**imilar-to-me = Rate people like you more favorably → "We went to the same school" → higher rating **Solutions:** → BARS (Behaviorally Anchored Rating Scales) → 360-degree feedback (multiple raters) → Frame-of-Reference training for raters → Forced distribution (bell curve ratings)

memoryAids.title: Telehealth Ethics & Practice
memoryAids.content: **Telepsychology requires clinical, technical, legal, and emergency competence.** 💻 Assess suitability, accessibility, technology limits, and alternatives for the individual client. 🔒 Use a documented privacy/security risk process. HIPAA does not “certify” products; covered providers may need appropriate safeguards and business-associate agreements. 📍 Authority to practice depends on where participants are located and current jurisdictional rules. PSYPACT may help eligible psychologists but is not universal permission. 🆘 Confirm location as clinically appropriate, plan for emergencies and disconnection, and know relevant local resources. 📋 Consent should address remote-service risks, privacy, records, fees, third parties, backup communication, and limits of the technology. Platform choice alone does not make telepsychology ethical or legally compliant.
memoryAids.reviewNote: Removed product-compliance shorthand and universal licensure claims; added risk assessment, authority-to-practice, emergency, and consent context.

memoryAids.title: Prenatal Development & Teratogens
memoryAids.content: **3 stages of prenatal development + key risks:** 🥚 **Germinal Period** (0-2 weeks): → Fertilization → implantation in uterine wall → Zygote → blastocyst → If teratogen exposure: all-or-none (miscarriage or no effect) 🫀 **Embryonic Period** (3-8 weeks): → MOST VULNERABLE to teratogens! → All major organs forming (organogenesis) → Heart, brain, limbs, eyes develop → Critical period for structural defects → "If it can go wrong, this is when" 👶 **Fetal Period** (9 weeks - birth): → Growth and refinement of existing structures → Still vulnerable but less so than embryonic period → Brain development continues (vulnerable throughout!) ⚠️ **Key Teratogens (EPPP favorites):** → **Alcohol** = #1! FAS: facial features, low IQ, growth deficiency → NO safe amount during pregnancy → **Tobacco** = Low birth weight, SIDS risk → **Rubella** = Deafness, heart defects, cataracts (1st trimester) → **Thalidomide** = Limb defects (historical) → **Lead/Mercury** = Cognitive deficits → **Cocaine** = Premature birth, irritability **Principles:** Dose-response, timing matters, genetic susceptibility varies
